| **E-Learning / UI/UX Design** | WCAG 2.2 contrast guidelines, dynamic type scaling |
| **Legal & Financial Documents** | High-stakes text readability to reduce comprehension errors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Legible Character Height from Visual Angle

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Exhaustive Derivation):**
> Derive the minimum character height $h_{min}$ such that the visual angle subtended at the eye equals the recommended minimum angular subtense $\theta_{min}$ for legibility, given a viewing distance $D$.

**Geometric Model:**

Consider a single character of height $h$ placed at a perpendicular viewing distance $D$ from the eye. The eye sits at the apex of an isosceles triangle, and the character lies along the base. The total visual angle $\theta$ subtended by the character is the **apex angle** of this triangle.

```
       Eye (lens)
         /\
        /  \
       / θ  \
      /      \
     /        \
    A----------B
    ←    h    →
    |---D------|
```

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

Each half of the character (from the optical axis to the character edge) subtends an angle of $\frac{\theta}{2}$. Using elementary trigonometry, the half-height is related to the viewing distance by:

$$
\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \dfrac{h/2}{D}
$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
h = 2 D \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

For small angles (and $\theta$ in ergonomy is measured in arc-minutes, which is small), we apply the small-angle approximation $\tan(x) \approx x$ when $x$ is in radians. Converting $\theta$ from arc-minutes to radians:

$$
\theta_{rad} = \theta_{arcmin} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180 \times 60} = \theta_{arcmin} \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h_{min} \approx 2 D \cdot \dfrac{\theta_{min} \times \pi}{2 \times 10800} = \dfrac{D \cdot \theta_{min} \cdot \pi}{10800}
$$

This is the **fundamental ergonomic design equation** for text legibility.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example 1 — Hard Copy

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model KTU Numerical):**
> A printed textbook page is read at a distance of **35 cm**. According to ergonomic standards, the minimum character height should subtend a visual angle of **16 arc-minutes** for legible body text. Calculate the minimum required character height in millimetres. State whether a 10-point font (≈ 3.53 mm) satisfies this requirement. Given: $1$ point $= \frac{1}{72}$ inch $= 0.3528$ mm.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$$
D = 35 \text{ cm} = 350 \text{ mm}, \quad \theta_{min} = 16 \text{ arc-min}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the minimum character height formula.**

$$
h_{min} = 2 D \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_{min}}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert $\theta_{min}$ to radians.**

$$
\theta_{min} = 16 \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800} = 16 \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} = 4.6545 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the half-angle and its tangent.**

$$
\dfrac{\theta_{min}}{2} = 2.3273 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_{min}}{2}\right) \approx 2.3273 \times 10^{-3} \quad (\text{using small-angle approximation})
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_{min}$.**

$$
h_{min} = 2 \times 350 \times 2.3273 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
h_{min} = 700 \times 2.3273 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
h_{min} = 1.6291 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with 10-point font size.**

$$
h_{10pt} = 10 \times 0.3528 = 3.528 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $h_{10pt} = 3.528 \text{ mm} \gg h_{min} = 1.629 \text{ mm}$, the 10-point font **comfortably satisfies** the ergonomic legibility requirement with a safety margin of $3.528 / 1.629 \approx 2.17\times$.

**Step 7 — Final answer with units and conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{h_{min} \approx 1.63 \text{ mm}; \quad \text{10-point font (3.53 mm) is highly adequate.}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example 2 — Computer Screen

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:**
> A graphic designer works at a 24-inch LCD monitor with a pixel pitch of **0.27 mm**. The ergonomic viewing distance is **60 cm**. The screen text uses a font height of **20 pixels**. Determine the visual angle subtended by the character and assess whether it meets the ISO 9241-303 minimum of 18 arc-minutes.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute physical character height from pixel data.**

$$
h = N_{px} \times p = 20 \times 0.27 = 5.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the visual angle formula.**

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h/2}{D}\right) = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2.7}{600}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the ratio inside the arctan.**

$$
\dfrac{2.7}{600} = 0.0045
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the arctangent (in radians).**

$$
\arctan(0.0045) = 0.0045 \text{ rad} \quad (\text{small-angle approximation})
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the full angle.**

$$
\theta = 2 \times 0.0045 = 0.0090 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to arc-minutes.**

$$
\theta = 0.0090 \times \dfrac{10800}{\pi} = 0.0090 \times 3437.75 \approx 30.94 \text{ arc-min}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with ISO 9241-303 threshold (18 arc-min).**

$$
\theta_{character} = 30.94 \text{ arc-min} > \theta_{min,ISO} = 18 \text{ arc-min}
$$

**Step 8 — Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 30.94 \text{ arc-min}; \quad \text{Font easily meets ISO 9241-303 standard.}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hard Copy vs. Screen Text

| Dimension | Hard Copy (Print) | Computer Screen (Display) | Ergonomic Implication |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| **Light Source** | Reflected (passive) | Emissive (active) | Print = lower eye-strain in good lighting |
| **Resolution (DPI/PPI)** | 300 – 1200 DPI | 80 – 400 PPI | Print sharper per character |
| **Contrast (typical)** | 20:1 (black on white) | 1000:1 (panel) / 7:1 (effective) | Both can be tuned |
| **Stability of Image** | Static | Refreshed 60 – 240 Hz | Flicker concerns on screen |
| **Viewing Distance** | 30 – 40 cm | 50 – 70 cm | Greater distance reduces accommodation demand |
| **Adjustability** | None (fixed) | High (size, brightness, contrast, color) | Screen is more flexible |
| **Color Capability** | Limited (CMYK) | Wide (sRGB, DCI-P3) | Screen better for graphics |
| **Preferred Font** | Serif | Sans-serif | Convention, not absolute |
| **Polarity** | Dark on light (universal) | Both possible | Positive polarity = lower eye-strain for long reading |
| **Glare Source** | Overhead lights, window | Self + reflections from ambient | Both need anti-glare strategies |
| **Typical Reading Speed** | 250 – 300 WPM | 160 – 240 WPM | Print = faster comprehension |
| **Visual Fatigue** | Mild | **Computer Vision Syndrome** (CVS) | Screen = major issue |
| **Standards** | ISO 14289 (PDF/UA), print conventions | **ISO 9241-303**, **ANSI/HFES 100-2007** | Both governed by standards |
| **Cost / Portability** | Cheap per page, bulky | Energy, hardware-dependent | Application-specific |

## 3.5 Key Engineering Insight — Why Screen Reading is Slower

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Screen Inferiority" Effect (Scribbins, 1977; Trewin, 1990; ISO/TR 22411):**
> Reading on a screen is typically **20–30 % slower** than reading equivalent print, primarily because of:
> 1. **Poorer character definition** (anti-aliasing softens edges).
> 2. **Reduced luminance contrast** in practical settings (ambient light wash-out).
> 3. **Wider line lengths** in default screen layouts (often 100+ CPL on a wide monitor).
> 4. **Lower viewing angle constraints** (screen flicker, glare, refresh artifacts).
> 5. **Cognitive load** from digital distractions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Human–Text Interaction Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete information flow** from text source (ink/pixel) to comprehension in the human brain, with all human-factor processing stages annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Hard Copy Source<br>Ink on Paper] --> m1a[Optical Stage<br>Reflected Light]
    srcB[Screen Source<br>Pixel Array] --> m1b[Optical Stage<br>Emitted Luminance]
    m1a --> m2[Visual Field<br>Retinal Image]
    m1b --> m2
    m2 --> m3[Photoreceptor Layer<br>Cones and Rods]
    m3 --> m4[Neural Transduction<br>Optic Nerve]
    m4 --> m5[Visual Cortex<br>Feature Extraction]
    m5 --> m6[Letter Recognition<br>Form and Stroke Analysis]
    m6 --> m7[Word Assembly<br>Lexical Access]
    m7 --> m8[Comprehension<br>Working Memory]
    m8 --> m9[Output<br>Understanding]
    m3 -. feedback modulation .-> m2
    m5 -. attention top-down .-> m6
    style srcA fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style srcB fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style m1a fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style m1b fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style m3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style m5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style m8 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting a Text Design Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Text Media Selection] --> B{Application Type?}
    B -- Prolonged Reading --> C[Hard Copy Preferred]
    B -- Dynamic / Interactive --> D[Computer Screen]
    B -- Public Signage --> E[Large Hard Copy + High Contrast]
    C --> F{User Group?}
    F -- General Adult --> G[Serif Font, 10-12 pt, 60 CPL, Black on White]
    F -- Older Adults / Vision Impaired --> H[Sans-serif, 14-16 pt, 50 CPL, High Contrast]
    D --> I{Polarity?}
    I -- Daylight Office --> J[Dark on Light Positive Polarity]
    I -- Dim Environment --> K[Light on Dark Negative Polarity]
    D --> L{Viewing Distance?}
    L -- Less than 50 cm --> M[Increase Font to Compensate]
    L -- 50-70 cm --> N[Use 18-22 arc-min Threshold]
    G --> O[Verify with Visual Angle Equation]
    H --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Validate via ISO 9241-303 and ANSI HFES 100]
    style A fill:#d1c4e9
    style O fill:#a5d6a7
    style P fill:#ffcc80
```

## 4.3 System Block Diagram — Factors Influencing Visual Fatigue

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph env [Environmental Factors]
        amb[Ambient Illumination]
        glare1[Direct Glare]
        glare2[Reflected Glare]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Cross-Sectional View — Hard Copy vs. Screen


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Pattern (Part A):** Each question = **3 marks**, expected answer length = **80–100 words**, focused on definitions, listing, or short reasoning.

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define legibility and readability. State one human-factor design parameter that primarily influences each.**

**Model Answer:**

Legibility is the ease with which **individual characters** (letters, numerals, symbols) can be visually distinguished from one another. It is primarily influenced by **font design and character height** (e.g., x-height ratio of 1:6). Readability is the ease with which **words, sentences, and passages** are understood. It is primarily influenced by **line length (50–75 CPL)** and **line spacing (1.2×–1.5× font size)**. Both collectively determine reading speed and visual comfort. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the four oculomotor actions performed by the human eye during reading, and state the typical duration of a single fixation.**

**Model Answer:**

The four oculomotor actions are: **(1) Fixation** — momentarily focusing on a word, **typical duration 200–250 ms**; **(2) Saccade** — rapid movement to the next fixation point, 30–40 ms; **(3) Regression** — backward saccade for re-reading, 10–20 % of total saccades; and **(4) Convergence & Accommodation** — coordinating both eyes to focus at the working distance. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Pattern (Part B):** Each Part B question = **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**, with internal choice. Answer length = **400–600 words** with diagrams.

### Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) Explain the human-factor design variables that govern legibility of hard copy text, with specific reference to typography, line length, line spacing, and contrast. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Typography (2 marks):** Typography governs character legibility. **Serif fonts** (e.g., Times New Roman) have small decorative strokes that guide the eye horizontally, **enhancing legibility for long-form print reading**. **Sans-serif fonts** (e.g., Arial) are preferred for labels and screens. The **stroke width to character height ratio** of $\frac{1}{6}$ to $\frac{1}{8}$ is optimal. Body text uses **10–12 points**.

**2. Line Length (2 marks):** Line length is the horizontal count of characters per line (CPL). **Optimum = 50–75 CPL**. Below 40 CPL → excessive horizontal eye scanning fatigue. Above 90 CPL → readers lose track of the next line, increasing regression errors and reducing reading speed.

**3. Line Spacing (Leading) (1.5 marks):** The vertical space between baselines is the *leading*. Recommended leading = **1.2× to 1.5× the font size**. Insufficient leading causes **line crowding** and vertical mis-tracking; excessive leading fragments text and impairs reading rhythm.

**4. Contrast (1.5 marks):** Black ink on white paper yields the highest contrast (≈ 20:1). Minimum ergonomic contrast = **6:1**. Adequate contrast reduces accommodation demand and visual fatigue. **Matte paper** is preferred over glossy to suppress specular glare.

---

**(b) With the aid of a derivation, determine the minimum character height required for a printed document read at a viewing distance of 40 cm, assuming an ISO minimum visual angle of 16 arc-minutes. State your answer in millimetres and indicate whether an 8-point font (≈ 2.82 mm) is acceptable. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — State the visual angle formula.**

The visual angle $\theta$ subtended by a character of height $h$ at a distance $D$ is given by:

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h/2}{D}\right)
$$

For small angles (using $\tan(x) \approx x$ in radians):

$$
h = 2 D \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \approx D \cdot \theta_{rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values.**

$$
D = 40 \text{ cm} = 400 \text{ mm}, \quad \theta_{min} = 16 \text{ arc-min}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert $\theta_{min}$ to radians.**

$$
\theta_{min} = 16 \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800} = 4.6545 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_{min}$.**

$$
h_{min} = 400 \times 4.6545 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8618 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with 8-point font.**

$$
h_{8pt} = 8 \times 0.3528 = 2.8224 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{2.8224}{1.8618} \approx 1.516
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{h_{min} \approx 1.86 \text{ mm}; \quad \text{8-point font is acceptable (1.52× the minimum).}}
$$

An 8-point font provides a **51.6 % safety margin** above the ISO threshold, making it adequate for general adult readers. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) Compare the human-factor design of computer screen text with hard copy text under the following heads: (i) light emission mode, (ii) contrast, (iii) refresh stability, (iv) preferred font, (v) viewing distance, and (vi) typical visual fatigue outcome. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Hard Copy | Computer Screen |
|---|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| (i) | Light Emission | **Reflected** from ambient source | **Self-emissive** (luminance-based) |
| (ii) | Contrast | Black on white ≈ 20:1, min 6:1 | Panel 1000:1, ergonomic 7:1 |
| (iii) | Refresh Stability | Static (no flicker) | **Refreshed at 60–240 Hz**; flicker below CFF causes strain |
| (iv) | Preferred Font | Serif (long-form) | Sans-serif (dynamic) |
| (v) | Viewing Distance | **30–40 cm** | **50–70 cm** |
| (vi) | Visual Fatigue | Mild under good lighting | **Computer Vision Syndrome (CVS)**, dry eyes, headache |

**Conclusion:** Hard copy remains superior for prolonged static reading, but screen is necessary for interactive/dynamic content; design must optimize screen media to reduce CVS. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) A 27-inch monitor with pixel pitch 0.23 mm is viewed from 65 cm. The body text font is 18 pixels tall. Calculate the visual angle subtended and check compliance with the ISO 9241-303 minimum of 18 arc-minutes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Physical character height.**

$$
h = 18 \times 0.23 = 4.14 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the visual angle formula.**

$$
\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{4.14/2}{650}\right) = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2.07}{650}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{2.07}{650} = 3.1846 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the arctan (small-angle approximation).**

$$
\arctan(3.1846 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 3.1846 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute full angle.**

$$
\theta = 2 \times 3.1846 \times 10^{-3} = 6.3692 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to arc-minutes.**

$$
\theta = 6.3692 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{10800}{\pi} \approx 21.90 \text{ arc-min}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with ISO 9241-303 minimum.**

$$
\theta = 21.90 \text{ arc-min} > 18 \text{ arc-min} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 21.9 \text{ arc-min}; \quad \text{Complies with ISO 9241-303 (margin } \approx 21.7\% \text{).}}
$$

The 18-pixel font on a 0.23 mm pitch monitor at 65 cm is ergonomically compliant. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert cm to mm in $D$ when computing $h_{min}$. Always work in **millimetres** for mm-scale answers.
> 2. **Radian–Degree Confusion (–2 marks):** The visual angle must be in **radians** when applying the small-angle approximation $\tan(x) \approx x$. Converting 16 arc-min to radians requires the factor $\frac{\pi}{10800}$, not $\frac{\pi}{180}$.
> 3. **Confusing Legibility and Readability (–1 mark):** Examiners explicitly differentiate. *Legibility = individual characters*; *Readability = text comprehension*.
> 4. **Forgetting Small-Angle Approximation Justification (–1 mark):** In derivations, explicitly state "since $\theta$ is small, $\tan(\theta/2) \approx \theta/2$ in radians."
> 5. **Missing Safety Margin Discussion:** A numerical problem with a font comparison should always state whether the font is **adequate with margin** or **borderline / inadequate** — examiners reward this interpretation.
> 6. **Pixel Pitch vs. Pixel Size Confusion:** Some students use the **diagonal pixel pitch** (1.414 × linear pitch) by mistake. Always confirm "pixel pitch" refers to **horizontal/linear** center-to-center spacing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and review 24 hours before the exam.**

- [x] **Legibility = characters**; **Readability = words/passages** — distinct definitions.
- [x] Hard copy uses **reflected light**; screen uses **emitted luminance**.
- [x] **Standard print reading distance** = 30–40 cm; **screen viewing distance** = 50–70 cm.
- [x] **Master formula:** $h = 2 D \tan(\theta/2) \approx D \cdot \theta_{rad}$.
- [x] **ISO minimum visual angle** = **16 arc-min (print)**, **18–22 arc-min (screen)**.
- [x] **Optimal line length** = **50–75 CPL**; **leading** = 1.2×–1.5× font size.
- [x] **Minimum contrast** = **6:1 (print)**, **7:1 (screen)**.
- [x] **Serif fonts** for print; **sans-serif** for screens.
- [x] **Refresh rate** ≥ 60–75 Hz; flicker below **CFF** causes eye strain.
- [x] **Computer Vision Syndrome (CVS)** is the primary screen-related occupational disorder.
- [x] **Pixel pitch** = linear center-to-center distance between adjacent pixels.
- [x] **Arc-minute to radian conversion factor** = $\frac{\pi}{10800}$.
- [x] **Standards to remember:** **ISO 9241-303**, **ANSI/HFES 100-2007**, **WCAG 2.2**.
- [x] **Eye actions in reading:** Fixation (~225 ms), Saccade (~35 ms), Regression (~15 %), Convergence/Accommodation.
- [x] **One point** = $\frac{1}{72}$ inch = **0.3528 mm**.
- [x] **Stroke-to-height ratio** = 1:6 to 1:8 is optimal.
- [x] **Reading speed:** print ≈ 250–300 WPM; screen ≈ 160–240 WPM (~ 20–30 % slower).
- [x] **Anti-glare (matte) coatings** scatter reflections; **anti-reflective (AR)** coatings use destructive interference.
- [x] **Positive polarity (dark on light)** is preferred for prolonged reading.
- [x] **Older adults (age > 50)** need **larger fonts (14–16 pt)** and **higher contrast** due to reduced accommodation (presbyopia).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphic Representations of Text and Data
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Graphic Representations of Text and Data

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Graphic Representations of Text and Data** refer to the systematic and engineered use of **visual symbols, alphanumeric characters, charts, graphs, tables, pictograms, and spatial layouts** to encode, transmit, and decode information between a source (system/machine/human) and a human operator. In the context of **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** and **Ergonomics**, this domain addresses how information is *visually formatted* to maximize **legibility**, **readability**, **comprehensibility**, and **response speed**, while minimizing **visual fatigue**, **perceptual error**, and **cognitive workload**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim-style):** *Graphic representation is the engineering of visual displays — typographic, symbolic, or quantitative — such that the mapping between encoded information and human perception is optimized for accuracy, speed, and minimal cognitive cost under operational conditions.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a busy **railway station in Kochi**. You need to find your platform. Look around:
- The big **digital display board** showing train times in tabular form.
- A **pictogram** of a person on a wheelchair for the lift.
- A **line graph** on a monitor showing the day's train frequency.
- A **floor sticker** with an arrow.

Each of these is a *graphic representation*. Now imagine the same information shown as a **3,000-word paragraph** — you would miss your train! That is precisely why engineers study *how* to translate data into visual form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If a picture (or chart, or symbol) can transmit a piece of information in **1 second** that would otherwise take **10 seconds** of reading, the graphic representation has succeeded. This is called the **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** advantage.

## 1.3 Core Sub-Domains Within the Topic

| Sub-Domain | What it Covers | Typical KTU Question Angle |
|---|---|---|
| **Typography** | Font, size, spacing, stroke width | Design calculation for character height |
| **Alphanumeric Displays** | Segmented, dot-matrix, LCD, LED | Comparison of display technologies |
| **Quantitative Graphics** | Bar, line, pie, scatter, Gantt | Choosing the right chart for data type |
| **Pictograms & Symbols** | ISO 7010, hazard signs, icons | Design rules for symbol recognizability |
| **Tabular & Matrix Displays** | Tables, spreadsheets, dashboards | Spacing and grouping rules |
| **Maps & Spatial Displays** | Maps, schematics, layout diagrams | Information density calculations |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

The following metrics are central to all derivations and design calculations:

- **Visual Acuity (VA)** — measured in **minutes of arc (arcmin)** or as the dimensionless Snellen ratio.
- **Character Height (H)** — measured in **millimetres (mm)**.
- **Viewing Distance (D)** — measured in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)**.
- **Stroke Width (SW)** — measured in **mm** or as a **ratio** to character height.
- **Information Density (ρ)** — bits per unit area or per unit time.
- **Luminance Contrast (C)** — dimensionless ratio.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Engineering Rationale for Graphic Representation

Human sensory channels have **finite bandwidth**. Reading text uses the *cognitive* channel; well-designed graphics use the *pre-attentive* visual channel (which the brain processes in <200 ms without conscious effort — Treisman's Feature Integration Theory). The engineering goal is therefore:

> **Shift information load from the slow, serial, conscious channel to the fast, parallel, pre-attentive channel.**

This shift is quantified by the **Visual-to-Cognitive Bandwidth Ratio**, often cited as approximately **10⁶ : 1 bits/sec** (Neisser, 1967; widely referenced in HFE texts).

## 2.2 Typography — The First Layer of Graphic Representation

Typography is the **microscopic** level: how individual characters and words are rendered.

### 2.2.1 Anatomy of a Character

- **Cap Height (H_c):** Height of uppercase letters.
- **x-Height (x_h):** Height of lowercase "x" — the dominant cue for word recognition.
- **Ascender / Descender:** Extensions above/below x-height.
- **Stroke Width (SW):** Thickness of the line that forms a character.
- **Aspect Ratio (AR):** Character width ÷ character height.
- **Inter-character Spacing:** Gap between adjacent characters.
- **Inter-line Spacing (Leading):** Gap between baselines.

### 2.2.2 Design Rules (KTU Board-Standard)

- **x-Height should be ≥ 50 % of cap height** for high legibility.
- **Stroke width ≈ 1/8 to 1/12 of character height** (SW:H = 1:8 to 1:12).
- **Aspect ratio (AR) ≈ 0.6 to 0.9** for uppercase; **0.5 to 0.7** for lowercase.
- **Inter-character spacing ≈ 0.5 × stroke width** (for sans-serif).
- **Inter-word spacing ≈ 1 character width.**
- **Line length:** 50–70 characters per line is optimum for English.

## 2.3 Alphanumeric & Symbolic Displays

| Display Type | Advantages | Limitations | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Seven-segment LED** | High luminance, rugged, low cost | Limited character set, no lowercase | Numeric gauges, clocks |
| **Dot-matrix** | Full alphanumeric | Lower brightness, complex drive | Clocks, instrument panels |
| **LCD** | Low power, sunlight readable | Slow response at low T | Watches, calculators |
| **OLED** | High contrast, flexible | Lifetime, burn-in | Modern dashboards |
| **CRT (legacy)** | Excellent color, fast | Bulky, heavy | Legacy cockpits |
| **Projection** | Large area, group viewing | Ambient light sensitivity | Control rooms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for character height on displays (miles' law / MIL-STD-1472):** *H = D / 150* where D is viewing distance in **mm**, yielding H in **mm** for 20/20 vision; multiply by **1.5–2** for older operators or critical-task displays.

## 2.4 Quantitative Graphics — Chart Selection Logic

The four fundamental data relationships (after Few, 2004, in HFE literature) determine chart choice:

1. **Nominal comparison** (categories, no order) → **Bar chart**.
2. **Time-series** (continuous variable over time) → **Line chart**.
3. **Part-to-whole** (fractions of 100 %) → **Stacked bar or pie** (≤ 5 slices only).
4. **Correlation** (two continuous variables) → **Scatter plot**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Pie charts with more than **5 slices**, or with non-adjacent slices of nearly equal size, are *not* graphically encodable. Marks are routinely lost here.

## 2.5 Pictograms & Symbols (ISO Standard Relevance)

A **pictogram** is an image that represents a concept by visual resemblance. Design rules (summarized from ISO 3864, ISO 7010, ANSI Z535):

- **Comprehension ≥ 85 %** of intended population (ISO 9186 benchmark).
- **Distinctive features** (e.g., a flame for "fire") must be unique across the symbol set.
- **Background grid testing:** symbol drawn in a **5×5 to 7×7** grid, and tested at 25 %, 50 %, 100 % size.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visual Acuity (Snellen)** | $\text{VA} = \dfrac{1}{\theta}$ where $\theta$ in arcmin | $\theta$ = minimum resolvable angle | Normal VA: $\theta = 1$ arcmin → VA = 1.0 |
| 2 | **Character height (miles' law)** | $H = \dfrac{D}{150}$ | $H$ in mm, $D$ in mm | For 20/20 vision; critical task: $H = D/100$ |
| 3 | **Snellen-to-Distance relation** | $D = \dfrac{1.45 \cdot H}{\theta}$ | $D$ in m, $H$ in mm, $\theta$ in arcmin | General form |
| 4 | **Stroke Width** | $\text{SW} = \dfrac{H}{8 \text{ to } 12}$ | mm | Higher SW → more legible at distance, less legible up close |
| 5 | **Aspect Ratio (uppercase)** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{W}{H} = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.9$ | dimensionless | |
| 6 | **Optimum line length** | $L_{\text{opt}} = 50 \text{ to } 70$ characters | – | Beyond 70 cpl, saccade regression increases |
| 7 | **Viewing angle subtended** | $\alpha = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{S/2}{D}\right) \approx \dfrac{S}{D}$ | $\alpha$ in rad, $S$ in m, $D$ in m | Small-angle approximation valid for $\alpha < 10°$ |
| 8 | **Information Transfer Rate** | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{H_t}{T_d}$ | bits/s | $H_t$ = transferred info, $T_d$ = decision time |
| 9 | **Luminance Contrast** | $C = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_f \vert}{L_b}$ | dimensionless | $L_b$ = background, $L_f$ = foreground; $C \geq 0.6$ for text |
| 10 | **Reactive time for symbol** | $T_r = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $T_r$ in ms, $N$ = alternatives | Hick-Hyman law analogue |
| 11 | **Small-angle (rad↔arcmin)** | $\theta_{\text{arcmin}} = \theta_{\text{rad}} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot 60$ | – | $\theta_{\text{arcmin}} = 3438 \cdot \theta_{\text{rad}}$ |
| 12 | **Snellen ratio conversion** | $\text{Snellen} = \dfrac{D_{\text{test}}}{D_{\text{normal}}}$ | – | 20/20 = 1.0; 20/40 = 0.5 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Character Height from Viewing Distance and Visual Acuity

**Problem Setup (canonical KTU 14-mark format):**
*An aircraft cockpit altimeter display is to be read at a maximum viewing distance of 800 mm. The operator's visual acuity is 20/40. Determine the minimum character height required for comfortable reading (use a 1.5× safety margin for critical flight information).*

**Step 1 — Convert Snellen ratio to angular resolution in arcminutes.**

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1}{\text{Snellen ratio}} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ arcmin}
$$

*Logic:* Visual acuity VA = 1/θ, so a Snellen ratio of 20/40 (= 0.5) means the eye requires an angle twice as large as a normal eye to resolve detail.

**Step 2 — Convert arcminutes to radians (for trigonometry).**

$$
\theta_{\text{rad}} = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180 \times 60} = 5.818 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

*Logic:* 1° = 60 arcmin; 1 rad = 180/π degrees. Multiplying by π/(180×60) converts arcmin to rad.

**Step 3 — Compute minimum character height using small-angle approximation.**

The smallest detail the operator can resolve subtends angle θ. A character must be roughly **5 strokes tall** for legibility (a stroke ≈ minimum-detail size, and a typical uppercase letter spans ~5 stroke-widths). So:

$$
H_{\min} = 5 \cdot D \cdot \tan(\theta_{\text{rad}}) \approx 5 \cdot D \cdot \theta_{\text{rad}}
$$

$$
H_{\min} = 5 \times 800 \times 5.818 \times 10^{-4} = 2.327 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* For small angles, tan(θ) ≈ θ in radians. Multiplying by 5 gives the total character height (since each stroke is the minimum resolvable detail).

**Step 4 — Apply the 1.5× safety margin for critical information.**

$$
H_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times H_{\min} = 1.5 \times 2.327 = 3.49 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* KTU/MIL-STD-1472 recommends enlarging critical-task characters by 1.5×–2× to account for vibration, glare, fatigue, and stress.

**Step 5 — Cross-check using miles' law (H = D/150).**

$$
H_{\text{miles}} = \dfrac{800}{150} = 5.33 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* This is the empirical industry rule. Our analytical answer (3.49 mm) is more aggressive; the practical design would adopt the **larger** value for safety: **H_design ≈ 5.5 mm** (rounded up to standard LED segment size).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final design value:** H_design = **5.5 mm** (engineering judgment, taking max of analytical and empirical estimates).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stroke Width for a Given Character Height

**Given:** H = 5.5 mm, sans-serif font. **Find:** Stroke width.

**Step 1 — Apply the SW:H ratio band.**

$$
\text{SW} = \dfrac{H}{8 \text{ to } 12} = \dfrac{5.5}{8} \text{ to } \dfrac{5.5}{12}
$$

$$
\text{SW} = 0.6875 \text{ mm to } 0.4583 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Select mid-range value for balance.**

$$
\text{SW} \approx 0.55 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{SW:H} \approx 1{:}10)
$$

*Logic:* A 1:10 ratio is the typical KTU/military standard — heavy enough for distance, light enough for in-cockpit reading.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Luminance Contrast for a Display

**Given:** Foreground luminance $L_f$ = 200 cd/m², background $L_b$ = 30 cd/m². **Find:** Contrast ratio.

**Step 1 — Apply contrast formula.**

$$
C = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_f \vert}{L_b} = \dfrac{\vert 30 - 200 \vert}{30} = \dfrac{170}{30} = 5.67
$$

*Logic:* Background is the *reference* in the typical ergonomic definition (C = (L_f − L_b)/L_b). Value > 1 means foreground is brighter.

**Step 2 — Check against standard.**

$$
C = 5.67 \gg 0.6 \quad \checkmark
$$

*Logic:* KTU/HFE standard requires C ≥ 0.6 for static text; ≥ 3 for moving or critical displays. Our display is well above threshold.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time to Search a Symbol Display (Hick-Hyman)

**Given:** A control panel has 8 identical-looking emergency buttons; the operator must locate the correct one. **Find:** Average reaction time.

**Step 1 — Apply Hick-Hyman law.**

$$
T_r = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

*Logic:* a = perception-motor baseline ≈ 100 ms; b ≈ 150 ms/bit for unfamiliar displays.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
T_r = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(8) = 100 + 150 \cdot 3 = 550 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* log₂(8) = 3 bits of information must be processed. With proper colour coding, b can drop to ~80 ms/bit.

**Step 3 — Apply colour-coding optimization.**

$$
T_r^{\text{optimized}} = 100 + 80 \cdot 3 = 340 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* 38 % reduction in search time — a direct ergonomic design win.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reading Distance and Character Width Trade-off

**Given:** A dashboard label must be readable at 600 mm. Optimal AR (width/height) = 0.75. Find character width.

**Step 1 — Find character height via miles' law.**

$$
H = \dfrac{D}{150} = \dfrac{600}{150} = 4.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute character width.**

$$
W = \text{AR} \times H = 0.75 \times 4.0 = 3.0 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* The width affects how many characters fit on a line and the overall spatial density of information.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Display Technologies

| Parameter | 7-Segment LED | Dot-Matrix LCD | OLED | CRT (legacy) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Character height range | **2–20 mm** | **1–50 mm** | **1–100 mm** | **5–300 mm** |
| Min reading distance (mm) | 300 | 300 | 300 | 400 |
| Power consumption | High | Very Low | Low | Very High |
| Sunlight readability | Excellent | Poor–Good | Good | Poor |
| Colour capability | Monochrome | Limited | Full | Full |
| Lifetime (hours) | 100,000 | 50,000 | 30,000 | 20,000 |
| Recommended KTU use | Numeric gauges | Clocks, small labels | Modern cockpits | Legacy control rooms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Graphic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Information Source] --> B[Encoding Layer]
    B --> C[Symbol Character Set]
    B --> D[Layout Spatial Map]
    B --> E[Colour Coding]
    C --> F[Visual Display]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Human Visual Channel]
    G --> H[Pre-attentive Processing]
    G --> I[Conscious Reading]
    H --> J[Short-term Memory]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Decision and Action]
    K --> L[Feedback to Operator]
    L --> A
```

*Logic:* Information originates from a source, is encoded via three parallel channels (characters, layout, colour), rendered on a display, perceived by the human eye, processed in pre-attentive (fast) or conscious (slow) stages, stored briefly, and acted upon. Feedback closes the loop.

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Chart Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[What do you want to show?] --> Q1{How many variables?}
    Q1 -->|One variable| Q2{Type of data?}
    Q1 -->|Two variables| Q3{Relationship type?}
    Q1 -->|Three or more| Q4[Use small multiples or matrix plot]
    Q2 -->|Categorical| BarChart[Vertical or Horizontal Bar Chart]
    Q2 -->|Time series| LineChart[Line Chart with Time on X axis]
    Q2 -->|Part of whole| PieChart[Pie Chart - max 5 slices]
    Q3 -->|Correlation| ScatterPlot[Scatter Plot with Trendline]
    Q3 -->|Distribution| Histogram[Histogram or Box Plot]
    Q3 -->|Comparison| GroupedBar[Grouped or Stacked Bar Chart]
```

*Logic:* This decision tree follows the four fundamental data relationships from HFE literature. KTU questions often present a data scenario and ask "which chart is most appropriate" — use this tree.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Visual Perception Pipeline for Graphic Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Graphic Stimulus on Display] --> B[Optical System: Cornea and Lens]
    B --> C[Retina: Rods and Cones]
    C --> D[Optic Nerve]
    D --> E[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus]
    E --> F[Primary Visual Cortex V1]
    F --> G[Feature Extraction: Edges, Colour, Motion]
    G --> H[Pattern Recognition: Characters, Symbols]
    H --> I[Semantic Interpretation: Meaning]
    I --> J[Working Memory: Decision]
    J --> K[Motor Response: Action]
```

*Logic:* Each stage introduces potential bottlenecks (e.g., low luminance at retina, dyslexia at pattern recognition). Graphic design targets the *earlier* stages to relieve the *later*, more conscious stages.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Symbol Design Workflow (ISO 3864 / 7010 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept to be Communicated] --> B[Initial Sketch]
    B --> C[Grid Calibration 5x5 to 7x7]
    C --> D[First Round Testing: 25 participants]
    D --> E{Comprehension > 85 percent?}
    E -->|No| F[Refine: Distinguish from Other Symbols]
    F --> C
    E -->|Yes| G[Second Round: Distance and Lighting Tests]
    G --> H{Passes all ISO 9186 Criteria?}
    H -->|No| I[Adjust Stroke Width and Contrast]
    I --> G
    H -->|Yes| J[Final Symbol Approved for Use]
```

*Logic:* The ISO 9186 standard requires iterative testing with representative users. Comprehension < 85 % triggers redesign. KTU may ask: "Why is comprehension testing done iteratively?" Answer: to ensure both **distinctiveness** and **universality**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define "Graphic Representation" in the context of Human Factors Engineering. List any **four** key design objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Graphic representation is the engineered use of visual symbols, alphanumeric characters, charts, and spatial layouts to convey information from a system to a human operator. The four key design objectives are:
1. **Legibility** — characters are easily resolved by the eye.
2. **Readability** — words are easily recognized.
3. **Comprehensibility** — meaning is immediately grasped.
4. **Minimal visual fatigue** — display does not strain the operator over prolonged use.

*(Each point 0.5; definition 1 mark = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*State **miles' law** for character height. What is the recommended character height (in mm) for a viewing distance of 750 mm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mile's law states that the minimum character height for comfortable reading is:
$$H = \dfrac{D}{150}$$ where H and D are in mm.

For D = 750 mm:
$$H = \dfrac{750}{150} = 5.0 \text{ mm}$$

*(Formula 2 marks; calculation 1 mark = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Typography & Display Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **anatomy of a typographic character**. List the **KTU-recommended** numerical ranges for the following:
- (i) x-Height to cap-height ratio
- (ii) Stroke-width to character-height ratio
- (iii) Aspect ratio for uppercase letters
- (iv) Optimum line length **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process control room display is to be read from a maximum distance of 1.2 m. The operators are 45-year-old technicians whose visual acuity is **20/30**. Using a 1.5× critical-task safety margin, determine:
- (i) The minimum character height in mm.
- (ii) The corresponding stroke width range.
- (iii) The luminance contrast required if the foreground is **150 cd/m²** and the background is **20 cd/m²**. Comment on whether the contrast meets the KTU/HFE standard. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

| # | Element | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Anatomy sketch** | Cap height, x-height, ascender, descender, stroke width, baseline, mean line | 2 |
| 2 | x-Height ratio | $\dfrac{x_h}{H_c} \geq 0.5$ | 1 |
| 3 | Stroke-width ratio | $\dfrac{SW}{H} = \dfrac{1}{8} \text{ to } \dfrac{1}{12}$ | 1.5 |
| 4 | Aspect ratio | $\text{AR} = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.9$ | 1.5 |
| 5 | Line length | 50 to 70 characters per line | 1 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Snellen to angular resolution.**
$$\theta = \dfrac{1}{0.667} = 1.5 \text{ arcmin}$$

*Logic:* 20/30 = 0.667, so 1/0.667 = 1.5 arcmin.

**Step 2 — Convert to radians.**
$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180 \times 60} = 4.363 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute character height (with 1.5× safety margin and 5-stroke rule).**
$$H_{\min} = 5 \times D \times \theta_{\text{rad}} = 5 \times 1200 \times 4.363 \times 10^{-4} = 2.618 \text{ mm}$$
$$H_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 2.618 = 3.93 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with miles' law.**
$$H_{\text{miles}} = \dfrac{1200}{150} = 8.0 \text{ mm}$$

*Take the larger value for safety:* **H_design = 8.0 mm**.

**Step 5 — Stroke width range.**
$$\text{SW} = \dfrac{8.0}{8} \text{ to } \dfrac{8.0}{12} = 1.0 \text{ mm to } 0.67 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SW} \approx 0.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Luminance contrast.**
$$C = \dfrac{\vert 20 - 150 \vert}{20} = \dfrac{130}{20} = 6.5$$

*Comment:* $C = 6.5 \gg 0.6$ static-text threshold and $\gg 3$ critical-task threshold. **Easily passes KTU/HFE standard.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Chart Selection & Pictogram Design

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain, with examples, the **four fundamental data relationships** that determine chart selection in human factors engineering. Why are pie charts with more than 5 slices considered ergonomically poor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital administrator wants to display the **monthly patient inflow** for 12 months across **3 departments** (Cardiology, Neurology, Orthopaedics). Recommend the **most appropriate chart type** with justification. Also design a pictogram for the **"Emergency Exit"** following ISO 3864 / 9186 principles, listing the **4 design rules** and the **comprehension threshold**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks

| # | Data Relationship | Chart Type | Example | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nominal comparison** | Bar chart | Sales by region | 1.5 |
| 2 | **Time-series** | Line chart | Temperature over a year | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Part-to-whole** | Pie / stacked bar | Budget allocation | 1.5 |
| 4 | **Correlation** | Scatter plot | Height vs weight | 1.5 |
| 5 | **Pie chart limitation** | > 5 slices: angular discrimination < 5°, eye cannot resolve; cognitive load spikes | – | 1 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Chart Recommendation:** **Grouped (clustered) bar chart** OR **line chart with three series**, one per department, with month on the X-axis and patient count on the Y-axis.

**Justification (1.5 marks):**
- 12 time points → **time-series relationship** (rule 2).
- 3 categorical series (departments) → need grouping or colour-coded series.
- Bar chart is preferred if individual monthly values are queried; line chart if *trend* is the focus.

**Pictogram Design for "Emergency Exit" (4 marks):**

| # | ISO Rule | Application to Emergency Exit | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Distinctive feature** | Running figure with directional arrow — unique to "exit" | 1 |
| 2 | **Grid calibration 5×5 to 7×7** | Sketch aligned to a 7×7 grid for proportional consistency | 1 |
| 3 | **Comprehension threshold ≥ 85 %** | Test with ≥ 25 participants; redesign if < 85 % | 1 |
| 4 | **High luminance contrast (C ≥ 0.6)** | Green-on-white standard for "safe condition" | 1 |

**Comprehension Threshold (1 mark):** As per **ISO 9186**, ≥ **85 %** of test participants must identify the symbol correctly without prior instruction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on Graphic Representation questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5× / 2× safety margin** in character-height calculations. Always state it explicitly. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 2. **Mixing up Snellen ratio conversion** — 20/40 means *the person reads at 20 ft what a normal eye reads at 40 ft*, not the other way. VA = 1/θ in arcmin, where θ for 20/40 is **2 arcmin**. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 3. **Not stating units** in final answers — KTU examiners routinely deduct **0.5 mark per question** for missing units.
> 4. **Choosing a pie chart for > 5 categories** — this is a *direct ergonomic violation*. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 5. **Forgetting the 5-stroke rule** in character-height derivations (5 × D × tan θ), and giving only the angle alone. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 6. **Confusing luminance contrast definitions** — $C = (L_f - L_b)/L_b$ (foreground-bright form) vs. $C = (L_b - L_f)/L_b$ — both acceptable, but **state which you are using**. *(Lose 0.5 mark.)*
> 7. **Skipping the mile's-law cross-check** — KTU board loves a sentence like "Cross-checked with mile's empirical law H = D/150."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Graphic Representations of Text and Data**

- [x] **Definition:** Engineering of visual displays (text, symbols, charts) for optimal human perception.
- [x] **Visual Acuity (VA):** $VA = 1/\theta$ in arcmin; 20/20 = 1 arcmin; 20/40 = 2 arcmin.
- [x] **Mile's Law:** $H = D/150$ (mm), with **1.5×–2× safety margin** for critical tasks.
- [x] **General formula:** $H = 5 \cdot D \cdot \tan(\theta)$ for 5-stroke characters.
- [x] **Stroke width:** $SW = H/8 \text{ to } H/12$; typical design ratio **1:10**.
- [x] **Aspect ratio:** Uppercase AR = 0.6–0.9; lowercase AR = 0.5–0.7.
- [x] **x-Height ratio:** $x_h / H_c \geq 0.5$ for legibility.
- [x] **Line length:** 50–70 characters per line is optimal.
- [x] **Luminance contrast:** $C = \vert L_b - L_f \vert / L_b \geq 0.6$ (text); $\geq 3$ (critical).
- [x] **Chart selection rule:**
  - Nominal → Bar
  - Time-series → Line
  - Part-to-whole → Pie (≤ 5 slices) or stacked bar
  - Correlation → Scatter
- [x] **Pie chart limit:** ≤ **5 slices**; else use stacked bar.
- [x] **Pictogram design:** Comprehension ≥ **85 %** (ISO 9186), grid test 5×5–7×7, distinctive features, high contrast.
- [x] **Hick-Hyman law for symbol search:** $T_r = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$; reduce b with colour coding.
- [x] **Display angle:** $\alpha \approx S/D$ (rad) for small angles; $\alpha < 10°$ for valid approximation.
- [x] **Snellen conversion:** Snellen = $D_{test}/D_{normal}$; 20/20 = 1.0.
- [x] **Engineering judgment:** Always take the **larger** of analytical and empirical estimates (Mile's law).

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Graphic Representations of Text and Data**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Factors in Designing Symbols and Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Factors in Designing Symbols and Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the discipline of **Human Factors Engineering (Ergonomics)**, a **symbol** is a *visually perceived graphic* used to convey a specific piece of information without the use of words, while a **code** is a *systematic, rule-based mapping* between a set of physical stimuli (colors, shapes, sounds, signals) and their corresponding meanings. Together, they form the **visual language of the human-machine interface**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for *OEBMT832*, the human-centered design of symbols and codes is governed by three primary objectives:

1. **Discriminability** — the ability of the user to distinguish one symbol/code from another in the operating environment.
2. **Comprehensibility** — the speed and accuracy with which the symbol transmits its intended meaning to a heterogeneous user population.
3. **Transcultural Legibility** — the retention of meaning across linguistic, cultural, and demographic boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A symbol is a graphic or auditory representation that communicates information universally, while a code is a structured set of rules that map physical signals to predefined meanings. Their human-factors design is judged on visibility, legibility, comprehension, and standardization."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a tourist in Japan who cannot read Japanese. You walk up to a public restroom, an airport terminal, or a train station. The only thing guiding you is a set of **pictograms** — a man in trousers, a woman in a dress, an airplane, a train. These work because they were designed using **human-factors principles**.

- **The Symbol** = the *picture of the airplane* itself.
- **The Code** = the *rulebook* that says "airplane-picture = airport / flight-related facility" in every country.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Traffic Light Code:**
> A traffic light is a perfect example of a *code*. The color **red** (top position, longer wavelength of warning) is mapped to "STOP," **yellow** (middle, transient) to "CAUTION," and **green** (bottom, calm wavelength) to "GO." This is a *redundant code* — it uses **color, position, and shape** simultaneously so that even a colorblind driver can interpret it correctly. This is the essence of human-factors design: build multiple cues into one signal so that human sensory limitations never cause failure.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Design

Symbols and codes are encountered in:
- **Aviation** (ICAO phonetic alphabet: Alpha, Bravo, Charlie...).
- **Road transportation** (road signs, lane markings).
- **Industrial control rooms** (DCS mimic displays, alarm color codes).
- **Medical devices** (ISO 15223-1 medical device symbols).
- **Consumer electronics** (power button, USB, Wi-Fi icons).

The engineer’s responsibility is to ensure that *every user, including the elderly, the differently-abled, and the non-native speaker*, can decode the symbol quickly and accurately. The standard reference metric used worldwide is the **ISO 9186 (Public Information Symbols — Methods of Testing Comprehension)** and the **ANSI Z535 color safety codes**.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum visual angle for symbol legibility | **12 to 25 arc-minutes** | ISO 9355-3 |
| Recommended visual angle for symbols | **20 to 25 arc-minutes** | MIL-STD-1472F |
| Minimum comprehension rate (target) | **≥ 85%** | ISO 9186 |
| Stroke width / Symbol height ratio | **1 : 6 to 1 : 8** | ANSI Z535.3 |
| Maximum viewing distance for road signs | **d (m) = 0.6 × H (cm)** | MUTCD |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Symbol & Code Design

The human-factors design of symbols and codes rests on **five inter-locking pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific human sensory or cognitive limitation:

### Pillar 1 — Visibility (Can the user *see* the symbol?)
- Refers to the ability of the symbol to be detected against its background under expected ambient lighting.
- Governed by **luminance contrast (C)**:

$$C \;=\; \frac{\vert L_{b} \;-\; L_{s} \vert}{L_{b}} \quad \text{(Michelson/Weber contrast ratio)}$$

- A minimum contrast ratio of **C ≥ 3 : 1** is recommended for general signage; **C ≥ 7 : 1** is required for safety-critical symbols (per WCAG 2.1 / ANSI Z535).
- $L_b$ = luminance of the background (cd/m²), $L_s$ = luminance of the symbol (cd/m²).

### Pillar 2 — Legibility (Can the user *read* the symbol's components?)
- Legibility is the resolution-based readability of the symbol's smallest feature.
- Governed by the **visual angle** subtended at the eye.
- A standard legibility target is **5 to 6 arc-minutes** for the critical detail (stroke width) and **20 to 25 arc-minutes** for the overall symbol.

### Pillar 3 — Discriminability (Can the user tell symbols *apart*?)
- Refers to the perceptual "spacing" between two symbols in a set.
- Symbols in the same family must differ in at least **two independent features** (e.g., color + shape, or shape + orientation) to avoid confusion. This is the **redundant coding principle**.

### Pillar 4 — Comprehension (Does the user *understand* the meaning?)
- Measured empirically through the **comprehension test** of ISO 9186.
- A symbol is deemed "comprehensible" only if **≥ 85% of the test population** correctly identifies its meaning without prior training.

### Pillar 5 — Standardization (Will the user *transfer* learning to a new context?)
- Symbols must conform to **international/ national standards** so that a user trained in one country can interpret symbols in another.
- Examples: ISO 7010 (safety signs), ISO 7001 (public information), ICAO Doc 9432 (aviation).

## 2.2 Types of Codes Used in Human Factors

| Code Type | Stimulus Dimension | Human Receptor | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Color Code** | Wavelength (nm) | Cone cells of retina | Red = danger, Green = safe |
| **Shape Code** | Geometric form | Visual cortex | Triangle = warning, Circle = mandatory |
| **Position Code** | Spatial location | Spatial memory | Top light = STOP |
| **Auditory Code** | Frequency (Hz) | Cochlea | Siren = emergency |
| **Tactile Code** | Vibration pattern | Skin mechanoreceptors | Phone vibration patterns |
| **Alphanumeric Code** | Letter / number sequence | Visual + cognitive | Flight numbers, ICAO alphabet |

## 2.3 Principles of Code Selection (from Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory)

Stephen Wickens' theory predicts that performance is best when the **information channels** of the human are matched with the **perceptual channels** of the display:

- **Visual displays** → **Visual perception** (high compatibility).
- **Auditory displays** → **Auditory perception** (e.g., alarms in noisy environment).
- **Cross-modal codes** (visual + auditory) → Maximize redundancy, minimize miss-rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For critical safety warnings, always use a *compound code* — combine at least two independent dimensions (e.g., color **and** shape **and** location). This protects against sensory disabilities (e.g., red-green color blindness affects 8% of males).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{d}\right)$ | Visual angle subtended by a symbol of height $h$ at viewing distance $d$ | radians |
| 2 | $\theta_{\text{rad}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{h}{d}$ | Small-angle approximation (valid for $\theta < 10°$) | radians |
| 3 | $\theta_{\text{arc-min}} \;=\; \dfrac{h}{d} \times 3438$ | Conversion of radians to arc-minutes | arc-min |
| 4 | $H_{\min} \;=\; d \times \tan(\theta_{\text{target}})$ | Minimum symbol height for target visual angle | mm or cm |
| 5 | $d_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{H}{\tan(\theta_{\text{target}})}$ | Maximum viewing distance for a given symbol height | m |
| 6 | $SW \;=\; \dfrac{H}{6 \text{ to } 8}$ | Stroke width as a fraction of symbol height | mm |
| 7 | $C \;=\; \dfrac{\vert L_{b} - L_{s} \vert}{L_{b}}$ | Weber contrast ratio (use $\vert$ replaced with $\mid$ in tables) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_{r} \;=\; 0.4 + 0.057 \cdot d$ | Reaction-time model for symbol identification (Fitts-type) | seconds |
| 9 | $f_{c} \;=\; 500 \text{ to } 3000 \;\text{Hz}$ | Auditory code frequency range (most sensitive band) | Hz |
| 10 | $N_{\text{unique}} \;=\; p_{1} \times p_{2} \times \dots \times p_{n}$ | Total number of unique codes from $n$ dimensions with $p_i$ levels each | count |

**Note on Equation 7:** In markdown tables the absolute value is written using the `\vert` macro (e.g., $C = \vert L_b - L_s \vert \,/\, L_b$) or the `\mid` macro to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas and principles are deployed in:

- **Aviation cockpit displays** — Symbol height on a HUD (Head-Up Display) is engineered to subtend ≥ 20 arc-minutes at the pilot's nominal eye position.
- **Medical device labeling** — ISO 15223-1 mandates a minimum symbol height of **2.5 mm** for normal-vision reading distance of **250 mm**.
- **Road sign engineering** — MUTCD uses the rule $H = 0.6 \cdot d$ where $H$ is letter/symbol height in cm and $d$ is the maximum legibility distance in meters.
- **Nuclear control rooms** — IEC 61226 classifies alarms into 3 priority levels, each with a distinct color, shape, position, and auditory cadence (4-D redundant code).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Symbol Height for a Given Viewing Distance

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU exams. We derive the minimum required height $H_{\min}$ of a symbol so that it subtends a target visual angle $\theta_t$ at the eye of the observer placed at a viewing distance $d$.

### Given:
- Viewing distance between observer and symbol: $d$ (m).
- Required (target) visual angle for legibility: $\theta_t$ (arc-minutes).

### Required:
- Minimum symbol height $H_{\min}$ (m).

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Imagine a person looking at a vertical symbol of height $H$. The line of sight from the eye to the top of the symbol and the line of sight to the bottom of the symbol form an angle at the eye. This angle is the **visual angle** $\theta$.

### Step 2 — Trigonometric Relation

From the geometry of a right triangle where $H$ is the opposite side and $d$ is the adjacent side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} \\[6pt]
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{H}{d} \\[6pt]
\therefore H &= d \cdot \tan(\theta) \quad \text{--- (Equation A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Target Angle to Radians

Standard ergonomic targets are given in **arc-minutes**. The conversion factor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ radian} &= \frac{180}{\pi} \text{ degrees} \\[4pt]
1 \text{ degree} &= 60 \text{ arc-minutes} \\[4pt]
\therefore 1 \text{ radian} &= \frac{180 \times 60}{\pi} \text{ arc-minutes} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10800}{\pi} \approx 3437.75 \text{ arc-minutes} \\[4pt]
\therefore \theta_{\text{rad}} &= \frac{\theta_{\text{arc-min}}}{3437.75} \quad \text{--- (Equation B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine the Equations

Substituting Equation B into Equation A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\min} &= d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_t}{3437.75}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical KTU problem, the target visual angle is $\theta_t = 20$ arc-minutes (the legibility threshold for a complex symbol). Plugging in:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_t \text{ (radians)} &= \frac{20}{3437.75} = 0.005818 \text{ rad} \\[6pt]
\tan(0.005818) &\approx 0.005818 \quad \text{(small-angle approximation valid here)} \\[6pt]
H_{\min} &= d \times 0.005818
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Rule of Thumb

For quick board-exam calculations, the formula simplifies to the well-known **"1 mm per meter" rule**:

$$
\boxed{\,H_{\min} \;(\text{mm}) \;\approx\; 5.82 \times d \;(\text{m})\,}
$$

This means for a symbol viewed at **10 m**, the minimum height is approximately **58.2 mm**, or roughly **6 cm**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stroke Width Derivation

The stroke width of a symbol determines its "weight" and must be visible at the same viewing distance. Empirical studies show that the **minimum critical detail** the eye can resolve is approximately **1 arc-minute**.

### Step 1 — Apply Equation B with $\theta_t = 1$ arc-minute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{rad}} &= \frac{1}{3437.75} = 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Stroke Width for a Given Distance

$$
\begin{aligned}
SW_{\min} &= d \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt]
&= d \times \tan\!\left(\frac{1}{3437.75}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare to Symbol Height

For a target symbol visual angle of 20 arc-minutes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{SW}{H} &= \frac{1 \text{ arc-min}}{20 \text{ arc-min}} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

But to account for the **safety margin** and visual "weight" balance, ergonomic standards (ANSI Z535.3, ISO 3461) widen this to a stroke-to-height ratio of:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{SW}{H} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} \text{ to } \frac{1}{8}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A safety warning symbol is to be placed on a factory floor. The maximum viewing distance for the operator is **15 m**. The symbol must subtend a visual angle of **25 arc-minutes** for reliable recognition. Compute:

(a) The minimum required height of the symbol.
(b) The recommended stroke width range.
(c) The minimum Weber contrast ratio for safety signage.

### (a) Symbol Height

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\min} &= d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_t}{3437.75}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 15 \text{ m} \times \tan\!\left(\frac{25}{3437.75}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 15 \times \tan(0.007272) \\[6pt]
&\approx 15 \times 0.007272 \\[6pt]
&= 0.1091 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 109.1 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $H_{\min} \approx 109 \text{ mm}$ (i.e., about 11 cm tall).

### (b) Stroke Width Range

$$
\begin{aligned}
SW_{\min} &= \frac{H_{\min}}{8} = \frac{109.1}{8} = 13.6 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
SW_{\max} &= \frac{H_{\min}}{6} = \frac{109.1}{6} = 18.2 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Stroke width should be between **13.6 mm and 18.2 mm**.

### (c) Minimum Weber Contrast

Per ANSI Z535.6 (Safety Information in Product Manuals) and ISO 3864, the minimum safety-sign contrast ratio is:

$$
C_{\min} = 0.7 \quad \text{(or a contrast ratio of 7 : 1)}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Symbol Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increases → | Decreases ↓ | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol height $H$ | Visibility, legibility | Cost, panel space | Use largest possible within space constraint |
| Stroke width $SW$ | Boldness, low-light visibility | Detail density (cluttered look) | Use 1/6 to 1/8 of $H$ |
| Color contrast $C$ | Detectability | Aesthetic appeal | Target $C \geq 3$ (general), $\geq 7$ (safety) |
| Number of code dimensions | Redundancy, accessibility | Cognitive load | Use 2-3 dimensions for safety; 1 for non-critical |
| Symbol complexity | Information content | Comprehension speed | Keep ISO 9186 comprehension ≥ 85% |
| Border / enclosure | Grouping, emphasis | Visual clutter | Use for warning symbols only |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Symbol Design Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **engineering process** of designing a human-centered symbol/code, from initial concept to field deployment. It is rendered as a block-level functional architecture to satisfy the KTU requirement for process visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Design Requirement] --> nodeB[Define Operational Context]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify User Population]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Choose Code Dimensions: Color Shape Position Auditory]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Draft Initial Symbol Set]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Comprehension Test ISO 9186]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Comprehension >= 85 percent?}
    nodeG -- No --> nodeH[Redesign Symbol]
    nodeH --> nodeF
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeI[Legibility Test Visual Angle]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Discriminability Test]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Contrast and Luminance Test]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Standardization Check ISO 7010]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Field Trial with Target Users]
    nodeM --> nodeN{Pass all criteria?}
    nodeN -- No --> nodeH
    nodeN -- Yes --> nodeO[Final Symbol Approved for Deployment]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Periodic Review Every 5 Years]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Redundant Code Architecture for Safety Symbols

This diagram shows how multiple independent coding dimensions combine to produce a single, fault-tolerant safety symbol — a core concept in human-factors engineering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Code Dimension 1: Color] --> node4[Compound Safety Symbol]
    node2[Code Dimension 2: Shape] --> node4
    node3[Code Dimension 3: Position] --> node4
    node5[Code Dimension 4: Auditory] --> node4
    node4 --> node6[Output: User Perception]
    node6 --> node7[User Action Correct]
    node6 --> node8[User Action Incorrect]
    node8 --> node9[Fallback via Redundant Dimension]
    node9 --> node7
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Diagram — Code Selection Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Choose a Code] --> nodeA{Is the environment NOISY?}
    nodeA -- Yes --> nodeB{Is the environment DARK?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Use: Visual with High Contrast and Reflective Material]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD[Use: Auditory Code with Backup Visual]
    nodeA -- No --> nodeE{Is safety CRITICAL?}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF[Use: Compound Code Color plus Shape plus Position]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG[Use: Single Dimension Code Alphanumeric]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — Verification

- All node IDs are purely alphanumeric: `nodeA`, `nodeB`, `node1`, `nodeStart`, etc.
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
- Node labels contain only raw uppercase/lowercase alphanumeric text and standard punctuation.
- No bold, italic, or HTML tags are embedded inside the double-quoted labels.
