# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the terms *symbol* and *code* as used in human factors engineering. Differentiate between them with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **symbol** is a *visually perceived graphic element* that conveys a specific piece of information without the use of words. Example: a red circle with a diagonal slash through a cigarette — universally understood as "No Smoking."

A **code** is a *systematic, rule-based mapping* between a set of physical stimuli and their corresponding meanings. It is a convention, not a single image. Example: the **ICAO phonetic alphabet** (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie...) is a code that maps each letter of the English alphabet to a specific spoken word, used in aviation to avoid confusion between similar-sounding letters.

**Key Difference:** A symbol is a *single visual element*; a code is a *rule-set* that may use many symbols or other stimuli.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any three human-factors criteria that a well-designed symbol must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 criteria):**

1. **Visibility:** The symbol must be detectable against its background under expected ambient lighting. This is governed by the Weber contrast ratio $C = \vert L_b - L_s \vert \,/\, L_b$, with a minimum of $C \geq 0.3$ for general use and $C \geq 0.7$ for safety signs.

2. **Legibility:** The smallest critical detail of the symbol must subtend a visual angle of at least **5 to 6 arc-minutes** at the eye, and the overall symbol must subtend **20 to 25 arc-minutes** for reliable identification.

3. **Comprehensibility:** As per ISO 9186, the symbol must be correctly identified by at least **85% of a representative test population** without prior training. A symbol that requires explanation fails this criterion.

(Other valid criteria: Discriminability, Standardization, Transcultural legibility.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) Discuss the human-factors principles involved in the design of symbols and codes. Include in your answer the five pillars of symbol design, the role of redundant coding, and the relevance of standards like ISO 9186 and ANSI Z535.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The design of symbols and codes is a sub-discipline of human factors engineering concerned with the optimization of visual information transfer between a system and its human operator. The design is judged on five pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Visibility:** The symbol must be physically detectable in the operating environment. This is engineered through sufficient luminance contrast, correct sizing for the viewing distance, and appropriate color choice. The Weber contrast ratio $C = \vert L_b - L_s \vert \,/\, L_b$ must exceed **0.3** for general signs and **0.7** for safety-critical signs (per ANSI Z535).

**Pillar 2 — Legibility:** The smallest feature (stroke width) of the symbol must subtend a visual angle of at least **1 arc-minute** at the eye, with a recommended minimum of **5–6 arc-minutes** for comfortable reading. The overall symbol height should subtend **20–25 arc-minutes** (per ISO 9355-3 and MIL-STD-1472F). This gives the practical rule: $H_{\min} = 5.82 \times d$ (mm per meter of viewing distance).

**Pillar 3 — Discriminability:** Symbols in a set must be sufficiently distinct that they cannot be confused. The principle of *redundant coding* (Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory) states that two symbols should differ in **at least two independent perceptual dimensions** (e.g., color **and** shape, or shape **and** orientation). This protects users with sensory limitations such as color blindness (8% of male population).

**Pillar 4 — Comprehensibility:** ISO 9186 defines a test protocol in which a representative sample of users (n ≥ 50) is shown the symbol without context and asked to identify its meaning. A symbol is *comprehensible* if **≥ 85%** of users correctly identify it. Below this, the symbol must be redesigned.

**Pillar 5 — Standardization:** International standards (ISO 7010 for safety, ISO 7001 for public information, ICAO Doc 9432 for aviation) provide a library of tested symbols. Using these standards is a human-factors best practice because it leverages the user's prior learning and enables cross-cultural transfer.

**Role of Redundant Coding:** Critical safety information should never rely on a single coding dimension. A red flashing alarm light, for example, is a compound code: it uses **color** (red = danger), **temporal pattern** (flashing = urgent), and **position** (typically ceiling-mounted). Even if the user is colorblind, the flashing pattern and ceiling location still convey the warning.

**Relevance of Standards:** ISO 9186 governs comprehension testing; ANSI Z535 governs the color, shape, and word-message format of safety signs in North America; ISO 3864 governs the geometric shape of safety signs (circle = mandatory, triangle = warning, square = safe condition). Adherence ensures legal compliance, cross-cultural legibility, and reduced training cost.

---

**(b) A safety warning symbol on an industrial machine must be legible from a maximum operator distance of 8 m. If the symbol must subtend a visual angle of 24 arc-minutes, compute: (i) the minimum required symbol height in mm, (ii) the recommended stroke width range, and (iii) the maximum allowable legibility distance if the actual symbol height on the machine is 75 mm.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Minimum symbol height:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_t \text{ (radians)} &= \frac{24}{3437.75} = 6.981 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \\[6pt]
H_{\min} &= d \times \tan(\theta_t) \\[6pt]
&= 8 \text{ m} \times \tan(6.981 \times 10^{-3}) \\[6pt]
&\approx 8 \times 6.981 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(small-angle approx.)} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.0558 \text{ m} = 55.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $H_{\min} \approx 56 \text{ mm}$.

**(ii) Recommended stroke width range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SW_{\min} &= \frac{H_{\min}}{8} = \frac{55.8}{8} = 6.98 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
SW_{\max} &= \frac{H_{\min}}{6} = \frac{55.8}{6} = 9.30 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $SW \in [6.98, \;9.30] \text{ mm}$, i.e., **7 mm to 9.3 mm**.

**(iii) Maximum legibility distance for $H = 75$ mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= \frac{H}{\tan(\theta_t)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.075}{\tan(6.981 \times 10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&\approx \frac{0.075}{6.981 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 10.74 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $d_{\max} \approx 10.74 \text{ m}$.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) Explain the concept of a *redundant code* with a suitable example. Discuss why redundant coding is considered a human-factors best practice in safety-critical systems like aviation and nuclear control rooms.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **redundant code** is a coding scheme in which a single piece of information is conveyed through **two or more independent perceptual channels simultaneously**. The purpose is to ensure that if one channel fails (due to sensory disability, environmental interference, or hardware fault), the user can still extract the correct information from the remaining channel(s).

**Example — Aircraft Stall Warning:**
Aircraft stall warnings are a classic 3-channel redundant code:
1. **Auditory** — a loud, intermittent horn (frequency ≈ 300–1000 Hz) at a specific cadence.
2. **Tactile** — a stick-shaker vibrating the control column at ~25 Hz.
3. **Visual** — a flashing red "STALL" annunciator on the Primary Flight Display.

The pilot receives the warning through three independent sensory pathways. A deaf pilot will still feel the stick-shaker; a blind pilot (IFR) will still hear the horn; a pilot in a noisy cockpit will still feel the vibration. **No single sensory deficit can mask the warning.**

**Why Redundant Coding is a Best Practice in Safety-Critical Systems:**

1. **Human Sensory Limitations:** Approximately 8% of males have red-green color blindness. A purely color-coded warning would be invisible to them. Adding shape and position coding overcomes this.

2. **Environmental Degradation:** In a smoke-filled room, dim emergency lighting, or a high-noise factory, any single channel may fail. Redundancy ensures survivability of the signal.

3. **Cognitive Load Reduction:** In a high-stress emergency (e.g., a pilot facing a stall), cognitive processing slows. A redundant code reaches the brain through parallel sensory pathways, reducing reaction time and decision error.

4. **Regulatory Mandate:** Standards like **IEC 61226** (Nuclear Power Plant — Instrumentation and Control), **ISO 7010** (Safety Signs), and **FAR 25.729** (Aircraft Warning Systems) **explicitly require** redundant coding for safety-critical alerts. Non-compliance can lead to loss of certification.

5. **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory:** This model predicts that performance is maximized when information channels are matched to user perception modalities and the load is distributed across them. Redundant coding implements exactly this principle.

**Industrial example — Nuclear Control Room Alarms:** Per IEC 61226, alarms are coded on **four dimensions** simultaneously:
- Color (red = highest priority).
- Shape (octagonal = evacuation, circular = abnormal).
- Position (top of annunciator panel = most recent).
- Auditory cadence (continuous tone = highest priority).

A control-room operator who is momentarily looking elsewhere will still hear the alarm; one in a noisy moment will still see the flashing color; one who is colorblind will still see the position and shape change.

---

**(b) A road sign uses a triangular warning symbol. The symbol is 90 cm tall, painted on a yellow background. If the average luminance of the yellow background is 250 cd/m² and the black symbol's luminance is 18 cd/m², calculate: (i) the Weber contrast ratio, (ii) the maximum viewing distance for a target visual angle of 22 arc-minutes, and (iii) state whether the contrast meets the ANSI Z535 minimum safety-sign requirement.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Weber contrast ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{\vert L_b - L_s \vert}{L_b} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\vert 250 - 18 \vert}{250} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{232}{250} \\[6pt]
&= 0.928
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $C = 0.928$ (a contrast ratio of approximately **92.8 : 1**).

**(ii) Maximum viewing distance for $\theta_t = 22$ arc-minutes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_t \text{ (radians)} &= \frac{22}{3437.75} = 6.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \\[6pt]
d_{\max} &= \frac{H}{\tan(\theta_t)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.90}{\tan(6.40 \times 10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&\approx \frac{0.90}{6.40 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 140.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $d_{\max} \approx 140.6 \text{ m}$.

**(iii) ANSI Z535 compliance check:**

ANSI Z535 requires a minimum contrast ratio of $C \geq 0.7$ (i.e., 7 : 1) for safety signage. Our computed value is $C = 0.928$, which is **well above 0.7**.

**Answer:** ✅ **Yes, the contrast meets the ANSI Z535 minimum safety-sign requirement.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing degrees and arc-minutes in the visual-angle formula.** The conversion is $1° = 60$ arc-minutes and $1 \text{ rad} = 3437.75$ arc-minutes. Many students forget the factor of 60 and get answers off by a factor of 60.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation validity.** The formula $\tan(\theta) \approx \theta$ is valid only for $\theta < 10°$. For typical symbol sizes (a few arc-minutes to a few degrees), it is valid. For wide-angle displays (e.g., car dashboards at 30°), it is not.
>
> 3. **Using Weber contrast incorrectly.** The denominator is the *background* luminance, not the symbol's. Also, in markdown, students often write `|Lb - Ls|` inside a table cell and break the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the 85% comprehension threshold.** A symbol that is *visible* and *legible* may still fail because it is not *comprehensible* (e.g., a stylized drawing of a wave might mean "water" to a Westerner but "fish" to a coastal Asian community). Always design for the *lowest-common-denominator* user.
>
> 5. **Mixing up "redundant code" with "duplicate code."** A *duplicate* code is the same channel used twice (useless); a *redundant* code uses *different* channels. This is a 2-mark trap question.
>
> 6. **Skipping the units in the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unitless numerical answers. Always write "**mm**", "**m**", or "**arc-minutes**".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **symbol** is a single graphic; a **code** is a rule-based mapping of stimuli to meanings.
- ✅ The **five pillars** of symbol design are: **Visibility, Legibility, Discriminability, Comprehensibility, Standardization.**
- ✅ **Weber contrast ratio:** $C = \vert L_b - L_s \vert / L_b$. General: $C \geq 0.3$; Safety: $C \geq 0.7$.
- ✅ **Visual angle formula:** $\theta = \arctan(H / d) \approx H / d$ for small angles. Convert arc-minutes to radians by dividing by **3437.75**.
- ✅ **Symbol height rule:** $H_{\min} \approx 5.82 \times d$ (in mm and m respectively) for a 20 arc-minute target.
- ✅ **Stroke width rule:** $SW / H = 1/6$ to $1/8$ per ANSI Z535.3 and ISO 3461.
- ✅ **Comprehension threshold:** $\geq 85\%$ of users (per ISO 9186).
- ✅ **Redundant coding** = using $\geq 2$ independent perceptual channels (color **and** shape **and** position) for safety-critical info.
- ✅ **Color blindness affects 8% of males** — never rely on color alone.
- ✅ **Reaction time for symbol identification** is given by Fitts'-type model: $t_r = 0.4 + 0.057 \cdot d$ (seconds).
- ✅ **Key standards to remember:** ISO 7010 (safety signs), ISO 7001 (public info), ISO 3864 (shape conventions), ISO 9186 (comprehension testing), ANSI Z535 (US safety signs), ICAO Doc 9432 (aviation), IEC 61226 (nuclear alarms).
- ✅ **Shape convention (ISO 3864):** Circle = mandatory; Triangle (equilateral, black on yellow) = warning; Square (green) = safe condition; Red circle with diagonal slash = prohibition.
- ✅ **Auditory code range:** $f_c = 500 - 3000$ Hz (the human ear's most sensitive band).
- ✅ For board problems, always **show all three steps**: angle conversion → formula substitution → numerical evaluation with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Displays - Qualitative Visual Displays
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Visual Displays - Qualitative Visual Displays

# Visual Displays — Qualitative Displays

## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Qualitative Visual Displays** are those visual display devices that communicate the **state, condition, direction, or existence** of a system variable without conveying precise numerical magnitude. They inform the operator **"what"** is happening and **"which way"** it is going, but not **"how much"** the variable has changed.

In human factors engineering, displays are broadly classified into three categories:

| Display Category | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantitative** | Shows exact numerical magnitude | Digital voltmeter, 7-segment readout |
| **Qualitative** | Shows only the state / direction / status | Indicator lamp, go/no-go gauge |
| **Check / Representational** | Shows system configuration | Schematic mimic panel, synoptic diagram |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine driving a car. The fuel gauge is a **quantitative** display (it shows exact litres remaining). However, the small engine-shaped **warning lamp on the dashboard** that simply lights up red saying *"engine has a problem"* — that is a **qualitative display**. It does not tell you *what* the fault is or *how serious* it is numerically; it only says *"something is wrong, attend to it."* The red/amber/green traffic signal on a road is another classic qualitative display — it tells you whether to GO, CAUTION, or STOP without measuring traffic density in numbers.

### 1.3 Key Design Metrics (KTU Board-relevant)

- **Wavelength range for human vision:** approximately **380 nm – 780 nm** (peak photopic sensitivity at ~**555 nm**).
- **Standard colour coding (per ANSI Z535 / IEC 60073 & KTU syllabus):**
  - **RED** = Danger / Emergency / Stop
  - **AMBER / YELLOW** = Caution / Abnormal / Warning
  - **GREEN** = Normal / Safe / Run / Go
  - **BLUE** = Mandatory action / Information
  - **WHITE / CLEAR** = Neutral / Confirmation

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Qualitative Visual Displays

Qualitative displays are broadly divided by the **physical principle** they use to encode information:

1. **Luminous Displays (Lights)**
   - Indicator lights
   - Signal / warning lights
   - Illuminated pushbuttons
2. **Flag & Tape Displays**
   - Rotating drum / flag indicators
   - Tape indicators (e.g., weather charts)
3. **Shape & Symbol Displays**
   - Pictorial displays
   - Symbolic status boards
4. **Simple Meter-type Qualitative Indicators**
   - Go / No-go gauges
   - High-low-normal band gauges
5. **Graphical & Bar Displays (showing direction or trend only)**

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Why and How

**Step 1 — The operator's cognitive need:** Before deciding an action, the operator first wants to know whether the system is in a *normal* or *abnormal* state. Precise magnitude is secondary.

**Step 2 — Encoding:** The designer encodes system state using one or more **display dimensions**:
- **Colour** (most powerful — pre-attentive processing by the eye)
- **Brightness** (high vs low intensity)
- **Flash rate** (steady vs flashing)
- **Position** (top/bottom, left/right of a panel)
- **Shape / Symbol** (triangle for warning, circle for info, square for mandatory)
- **Size** (larger = higher priority)

**Step 3 — Perception:** The eye's retinal **cone cells** (responsible for colour vision under photopic conditions, > 3 cd/m²) decode the wavelength. **Rod cells** decode brightness under scotopic conditions. Hence **colour coding works only in well-lit environments**.

**Step 4 — Decision:** Operator maps the perceived stimulus to a stored mental model and takes action.

### 2.3 Principles of Indicator Light Design (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Recommendation | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Colour code | RED = danger, AMBER = caution, GREEN = safe | International standard, ANSI Z535 |
| Flash vs steady | Flash = attention / urgency; steady = status | Flash at **3–10 Hz** (most attention-grabbing) is recommended |
| Flash rate optimal | **3–10 Hz**, best at **5–7 Hz** | Matches brain's alpha-wave response; <3 Hz perceived as flicker, >10 Hz risks photosensitive epilepsy |
| Luminance contrast | Display should be **at least 2×** surrounding panel luminance | Ensures pop-out visibility |
| Viewing distance | Label text size = **viewing distance / 200** (m) minimum | Snellen-based legibility rule |
| Number of indicator lights | **Maximum 6** in a single functional group | Miller's rule (7 ± 2) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum character height (mm)} &= \frac{\text{Viewing distance (mm)}}{200}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimal flash rate for attention} &\approx 5 \text{ to } 7 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Display contrast ratio} &\geq 2:1 \text{ (luminance ratio)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Qualitative displays dominate the cockpit of any aircraft, the dashboard of a nuclear reactor, and modern SCADA/ICS HMI screens. They reduce operator cognitive load and shorten **Mean-Time-To-Respond (MTTR)** during emergencies because the eye detects colour and motion faster than reading digits.

### 2.5 Bar-Chart Qualitative Displays

A bar whose **length** changes proportionally is technically quantitative. However, the qualitative **bar display** simply indicates whether a parameter is **within band**, **rising**, or **falling**, often using colour-coded zones:

- Green zone = safe operating region
- Amber zone = caution region
- Red zone = danger region

The **direction of needle/bar movement** also conveys qualitative trend information (rising ↗, falling ↘, steady →).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Qualitative Display Types

| Display Type | Information Conveyed | Encoding Variable | Best Used When | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indicator light | State (on/off) | Colour + on/off | Binary status needed | Power ON, pump running |
| Warning light | Urgent abnormal state | Red colour + flash | Emergency attention required | Fire alarm, low oil pressure |
| Illuminated pushbutton | Command acknowledgement | Colour + position | Operator must confirm an action | "START", "STOP" buttons on PLC |
| Flag indicator | Direction / state | Mechanical flag position | Discrete level states | Old radar PPI, valve position |
| Pictorial display | Spatial / functional state | Recognisable image | System overview needed | Cockpit engine synoptic, mimic panel |
| Bar-zone gauge | Within / out of range | Colour zones on bar | Operator needs to know band | Boiler pressure, temperature |
| Trend indicator | Direction of change | Arrow / slope | Process behaviour monitoring | Control room strip chart |

### 3.2 Derivation — Why 5–7 Hz is Optimal Flash Frequency

Human visual system flicker fusion occurs above a critical frequency $f_c$ above which flashing appears as steady light.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c &\approx 60 \text{ Hz (photopic, high luminance)} \\
f_c &\approx 25 \text{ Hz (mesopic, moderate)} \\
f_c &\approx 15 \text{ Hz (scotopic, low luminance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **maximum attention capture** without crossing into fusion, designers use a frequency well **below** $f_c$ but **above** the threshold where the brain ignores the flicker (~2–3 Hz).

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{optimal}} &\in [3 \text{ Hz},\ 10 \text{ Hz}] \\
f_{\text{recommended, KTU syllabus}} &\in [5 \text{ Hz},\ 7 \text{ Hz}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning for the range:**
- **Below 3 Hz** → perceived as discrete blinks, less alarming, often ignored.
- **5–7 Hz** → matches cortical alpha-wave band, hence strongest involuntary orienting response.
- **Above 10 Hz** → begins approaching flicker fusion in well-lit panels; also poses photosensitive epilepsy risk (WHO guideline: avoid flashing > 3 Hz in public displays).

### 3.3 Derivation — Minimum Character / Symbol Size

For a label associated with a qualitative display (e.g., "OIL PRESS LOW"):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\min} &= \frac{D}{200} \\
\text{where } H_{\min} &= \text{minimum character height (m)} \\
D &= \text{operator viewing distance (m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):** An operator's eye is **800 mm** away from a warning lamp label.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\min} &= \frac{0.800}{200} \\
H_{\min} &= 0.004 \text{ m} \\
H_{\min} &= 4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the smallest legible character is **4 mm tall**. Designers round up to **5 mm** for safety margin.

### 3.4 Coding Logic — Colour Hierarchy

The International Standard **IEC 60073** codifies the meaning of colours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RED} &\rightarrow \text{Danger — act immediately} \\
\text{AMBER/YELLOW} &\rightarrow \text{Caution — act soon, abnormal state} \\
\text{GREEN} &\rightarrow \text{Safe — normal operation, run permitted} \\
\text{BLUE} &\rightarrow \text{Mandatory — action required by operator} \\
\text{WHITE/CLEAR/GREY} &\rightarrow \text{Neutral — no specific meaning, info}
\end{aligned}
$$

This hierarchy reduces **operator decision time** because the colour itself triggers a pre-trained response, bypassing language processing.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classification of Qualitative Displays

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Qualitative Visual Displays"]
    root --> lights["Luminous Displays"]
    root --> mech["Mechanical / Flag Displays"]
    root --> graphic["Graphical / Pictorial Displays"]
    root --> meter["Simple Meter-Type Indicators"]

    lights --> ind["Indicator Lights"]
    lights --> sig["Signal / Warning Lights"]
    lights --> illPB["Illuminated Pushbuttons"]

    mech --> flag["Rotating Flag Indicators"]
    mech --> tape["Tape Indicators"]

    graphic --> picto["Pictorial / Synoptic Displays"]
    graphic --> mimic["Mimic Diagrams"]
    graphic --> barZ["Bar-Zone Displays"]

    meter --> gng["Go / No-Go Gauges"]
    meter --> hl["High-Low-Normal Gauges"]

    ind --> cod["Encoding Variables: Colour, Flash Rate, Position, Size, Brightness"]
    sig --> cod
    illPB --> cod
    flag --> cod
    tape --> cod
    picto --> cod
    mimic --> cod
    barZ --> cod
    gng --> cod
    hl --> cod

    cod --> humanV["Human Visual System: Cones (Colour, Photopic) + Rods (Brightness, Scotopic)"]
    humanV --> brain["Operator Cognition: Mapping Stimulus to Mental Model"]
    brain --> action["Operator Action"]

    style root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style cod fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style humanV fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style brain fill:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style action fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Encoding Hierarchy of a Qualitative Display (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    input["System Variable Sensor"]
    input --> thresh["Threshold Comparator"]
    thresh --> decision{"State?"}
    decision -->|Normal| green["GREEN Lamp + Steady"]
    decision -->|Caution| amber["AMBER Lamp + Slow Flash 2 Hz"]
    decision -->|Danger| red["RED Lamp + Fast Flash 5-7 Hz"]
    green --> op["Operator: Continue Normal Operation"]
    amber --> op
    red --> op

    style red fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style amber fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style green fill:#385723,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style thresh fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define qualitative displays. Give two examples.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Qualitative visual displays are displays that present information about the **state, condition, or direction** of a system variable without indicating its **precise numerical magnitude**. They tell the operator *what* is happening rather than *how much* is happening.
> **Examples (any two, ½ mark each):**
> 1. Red warning lamp on a car dashboard indicating low oil pressure.
> 2. Green "Power ON" indicator on a control panel.
> 3. Traffic signal light (Red/Yellow/Green).
> 4. Pictorial cockpit synoptic showing engine status.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
*State the standard colour coding for indicator lights as per IEC 60073.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> As per IEC 60073 (also accepted by KTU syllabus):
> 1. **RED** — Danger, emergency, stop or immediate action required. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **AMBER/YELLOW** — Caution, abnormal condition, attention required. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **GREEN** — Safe, normal operation, proceed. *(½ mark)*
> 4. **BLUE** — Mandatory action or information. *(½ mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write "qualitative displays show numerical values" — **THIS IS WRONG** and costs 1 mark immediately.
> - When asked about colour coding, **always cite the IEC/ANSI standard**; do not invent codes.
> - Students often forget the **flash-rate range (5–7 Hz)** in warning-lamp questions. Examiners specifically award marks for the numerical value.
> - Do **not** confuse qualitative displays with **quantitative displays**; the KTU paper frequently asks for the difference table.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Explain the different types of qualitative visual displays with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Discuss the guidelines for designing indicator lights (colour, flash rate, brightness, position). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Qualitative Visual Displays (7 marks):**

1. **Indicator Lights / Signal Lamps (2 marks):**
   Simple on/off lamps used to indicate binary status. Example: green "RUN" lamp on a motor starter panel; red "OVERLOAD" trip lamp.

2. **Illuminated Pushbuttons (1 mark):**
   Combines a control switch and a status lamp. The lamp lights up to confirm the action has been registered. Example: "START" button in a CNC machine that glows green when the spindle is running.

3. **Flag / Tape Indicators (1 mark):**
   Mechanical rotating flags or moving tapes that show discrete states through visible position. Example: weather-station wind-direction flags; older radar PPI displays.

4. **Pictorial / Synoptic Displays (1.5 marks):**
   Schematic image of the system where coloured lamps or symbols show the state of each subsystem. Example: aircraft ECAM/EICAS synoptic displays, power-plant mimic panels.

5. **Simple Meter-type Qualitative Indicators (1.5 marks):**
   Go/No-Go gauges, high-low-normal band gauges with colour zones. Example: boiler pressure dial with green-amber-red zones where the needle position conveys only "safe / caution / danger" without precise reading.

**(b) Design Guidelines for Indicator Lights (7 marks):**

| # | Guideline | Recommendation | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Colour coding** | RED = danger, AMBER = caution, GREEN = safe (IEC 60073) | 2 |
| 2 | **Flash rate** | Steady = status; Flashing = urgency. Optimal attention flash = **5–7 Hz**; > 10 Hz risks fusion/epilepsy; < 3 Hz ignored | 1.5 |
| 3 | **Brightness / luminance** | Display must be at least **2×** surrounding panel luminance for pop-out | 1 |
| 4 | **Position on panel** | Most important / most safety-critical lamps placed in the **central foveal field** (within ±15° of line of sight) | 1 |
| 5 | **Size & spacing** | Sized for the viewing distance: $H_{\min} = D/200$ | 0.5 |
| 6 | **Grouping & labelling** | No more than **6 lamps per functional group**; each lamp must have a clear label | 0.5 |
| 7 | **Avoid colour under low light** | Use redundant coding (shape/symbol + colour) for colour-blind operators | 0.5 |

Total: **14 marks**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*(a)* Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative visual displays. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* What is the importance of colour coding in qualitative displays? Explain with reference to international standards. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Qualitative vs Quantitative Displays (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Qualitative Display | Quantitative Display |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Shows the **state / direction** of a variable | Shows the **exact numerical magnitude** |
| **Information conveyed** | "What" and "which way" | "How much" |
| **Reading precision** | No numerical reading required | Operator must read digits / scale |
| **Cognitive load** | Low — pattern recognition | Higher — needs numerical interpretation |
| **Response time** | **Faster** (pre-attentive) | Slower (attentive processing) |
| **Typical examples** | Indicator lamp, go/no-go gauge, traffic light | Digital voltmeter, ruler, thermometer, ammeter |
| **Error type** | Misinterpretation of state | Reading error / parallax error |
| **Use case** | Status monitoring, alarms, warnings | Process control, precise adjustments |

(Marks: 1 mark per valid contrasting row × 7 = 7 marks)

**(b) Importance of Colour Coding (7 marks):**

1. **Speed of detection (2 marks):** Colour is processed pre-attentively by the human visual system. The eye detects a red lamp in a sea of green within ~200 ms, far faster than reading a number. This is critical in emergencies.

2. **Reduced cognitive load (1 mark):** Operator does not have to remember the meaning of every label; the colour itself triggers a learned response (e.g., RED → STOP).

3. **International standardisation (2 marks):** Standards like **IEC 60073, ANSI Z535, IS 1904** prescribe the colour semantics, ensuring operators trained in one system can understand displays worldwide. This is vital for aviation, nuclear, and process industries with global workforce mobility.

4. **Redundant encoding for safety (1 mark):** Colour works alongside flash, position, and shape, so that even a colour-blind operator (~8% of males) can still decode the warning.

5. **Grouping & prioritisation (1 mark):** A consistent colour palette across the panel creates instant visual hierarchy — danger zone red immediately clusters the eye on critical alarms.

Total: **14 marks**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Qualitative Visual Displays**

- ✅ Qualitative displays show **state, direction, or condition** — **NOT** numerical values.
- ✅ Three broad categories: **luminous, mechanical, graphical/pictorial, simple-meter**.
- ✅ Standard colour code (IEC 60073): **RED = danger, AMBER = caution, GREEN = safe, BLUE = mandatory, WHITE = neutral**.
- ✅ **Flashing light = urgency**; optimal attention flash rate = **5–7 Hz** (do not exceed 10 Hz).
- ✅ Indicator luminance must be **≥ 2×** ambient panel luminance for pop-out visibility.
- ✅ Apply Miller's rule: **maximum 6 indicator lights per functional group**.
- ✅ Minimum character height for labels: $H_{\min} = D / 200$.
- ✅ Colour vision (cones) requires photopic lighting — use **redundant coding (shape/flash)** for low-light environments and for colour-blind operators.
- ✅ Bar-zone gauges convey qualitative "within band / out of band" information through **colour zones**.
- ✅ Pictorial / synoptic displays mimic the system geometry and let the operator grasp overall system state at a glance (used in aircraft ECAM, SCADA mimic panels).
- ✅ Difference from quantitative displays is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU ESE — memorize the contrast table.
- ✅ Always mention the **relevant international standard** (IEC 60073, ANSI Z535) when discussing colour coding to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantitative Visual Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quantitative Visual Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantitative Visual Displays** are engineered visual interfaces that communicate **numerical, measurable, or proportional information** to a human operator through controlled visual stimuli such as pointers, scales, numerals, graphs, or gauges. They are designed to allow accurate and rapid perception of **quantity, magnitude, rate of change, or directional deviation** of a system parameter.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these displays are classified as a sub-category of **Visual Displays** under *Visual-Display Systems* in Human-Machine Interaction (HMI). The core objective is to translate a physical variable (temperature, pressure, speed, voltage, etc.) into a **perceptual quantity** that can be read, compared, and acted upon by the operator.

The fundamental variables involved are:

- **Stimulus (S):** The actual physical value (e.g., 75 °C).
- **Display (D):** The visual presentation of S (e.g., a needle pointing to 75).
- **Response (R):** The operator's perceived/recorded reading.
- **Accuracy:** The closeness of R to S.
- **Precision:** The repeatability of R for the same S.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic belongs to **Module 2 – Visual Displays and Auditory Displays**, under the broader unit *Visual-Display Systems, Types of Displays, Quantitative Displays, Qualitative Displays, Check Reading, Check Displays, Warning Displays*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the speedometer on a motorcycle versus the digital speed readout on a modern electric scooter.

- On the motorcycle, a **needle sweeps across a circular dial**. You don't just read "60 km/h" — you *feel* that you are halfway through the dial, you sense acceleration as the needle climbs, and you can instantly compare to a known mark.
- On the e-scooter, the dashboard shows **"062"** in bright seven-segment LEDs. It's exact, but if you want to know "how fast am I accelerating?" you have to do mental subtraction between successive readings.

The motorcycle dial is a classic **analog quantitative display**; the LED readout is a **digital quantitative display**. Both are communicating the same *quantity*, but through very different perceptual and cognitive channels.

**Quantitative Visual Displays = "The Eyes of the Machine"** — they are the bridge between the invisible internal state of an engineered system and the operator's conscious decision-making.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Display Engineering

The following are the **standard KTU-recognized metrics** for evaluating any quantitative visual display:

- **Resolution:** The smallest detectable change in the displayed value.
- **Accuracy:** Mean deviation of observed reading from true value (usually expressed in %).
- **Precision (Repeatability):** Standard deviation of repeated readings.
- **Readability:** Minimum visual angle required for the operator to identify the value (typically measured in **arc-minutes**, with a benchmark value of **1 arc-minute** for threshold readability and **10–12 arc-minutes** for comfortable reading).
- **Legibility Distance:** Maximum distance at which the display can be correctly read.
- **Response Time:** Time lag between a change in physical variable and corresponding change on the display.
- **Update Rate (Hz):** Frequency at which the display refreshes (typically **≥ 30 Hz** to avoid flicker perception).
- **Luminance Contrast Ratio:** Ratio of display luminance to background, ideally ≥ **3:1** for daytime and up to **10:1** for high-precision tasks.
- **Viewing Angle:** Cone within which the display remains readable (typically **±15° to ±30°** from normal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** A common 3-mark question is *"Differentiate between quantitative and qualitative displays."* — Always mention that **quantitative displays give magnitude/numerical value**, whereas **qualitative displays indicate status or condition only** (e.g., "ON/OFF", "NORMAL/WARNING/CRITICAL").

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview of Quantitative Displays

Quantitative visual displays can be broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis 1 — Format of Presentation:**
1. **Analog Displays** (pointer-scale / dial / gauge)
2. **Digital Displays** (numeric readout / seven-segment / LCD digits)
3. **Hybrid Displays** (combination of both)

**Axis 2 — Information Density:**
1. **Check-reading displays** (yes/no, threshold, go/no-go)
2. **Quantitative-reading displays** (precise numerical magnitude)

The **reading task** itself is further divided into:
- **Static Reading:** Reading a fixed value (e.g., altimeter at a moment).
- **Dynamic Reading (Tracking):** Continuously reading a varying value (e.g., attitude indicator during a turn).

---

## 1.5 Reading Tasks in Human Factors

| Reading Task | Cognitive Demand | Typical Display Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantitative Reading** | High — requires precise numeric estimation | Digital scale, finely-graduated analog |
| **Check Reading** | Low — pass/fail judgment | Single warning light, bar indicator |
| **Tracking** | Continuous — pattern recognition over time | Analog dial with smooth needle motion |
| **Comparison** | Medium — relative judgment between two values | Twin needles, dual-scale dial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Students often confuse *check-reading* and *quantitative-reading* displays. A *check-reading* display (e.g., a green LED showing "system OK") tells you only that a value is within tolerance; it does *not* tell you the value. A *quantitative* display tells you the actual magnitude.

---

## 1.6 Why Quantitative Displays Matter in Engineering Design

Quantitative displays are deployed in virtually every engineered system:

- **Aviation:** Altimeter, airspeed indicator, vertical speed indicator, fuel quantity gauge.
- **Process Control (Refineries, Power Plants):** Pressure gauges, flow meters, thermocouple readouts, valve position indicators.
- **Automotive:** Speedometer, tachometer, fuel gauge, engine temperature gauge.
- **Medical:** Patient monitors (heart rate, SpO₂, blood pressure).
- **Nuclear / Defence:** Reactor power meters, radar displays, sonar consoles.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Battery percentage, signal strength bars (semi-quantitative), thermostat.

The **economic and safety implications** of poorly designed quantitative displays are enormous. Studies in aviation human factors attribute a significant percentage of controlled-flight-into-terrain (CFIT) and altitude-deviation incidents to **altimeter misreading**, a classic quantitative-display failure.

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Human–Display Information Chain

The communication of quantitative information through a visual display follows a strict perceptual-cognitive pipeline:

**Physical Variable → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Display Element → Visual Stimulus → Human Visual System → Perception → Cognitive Interpretation → Decision → Motor Action**

Every quantitative display must be designed so that the **bandwidth** of each stage is matched to the next. A transducer capable of ±0.01% accuracy feeding a display with ±5% readability is a **wasted engineering investment**.

The **psychophysical foundation** of quantitative display design rests on three laws:

1. **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I = k$, where $\Delta I$ is the *just noticeable difference (JND)* and $k$ is Weber's constant (≈ 0.08 for visual length, ≈ 0.03 for visual brightness, ≈ 0.05 for auditory pitch).
2. **Fechner's Law:** $S = k \cdot \log(I / I_0)$ — perceived intensity scales with the logarithm of stimulus intensity.
3. **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = k \cdot I^n$ — generalized form covering all sensory continua (e.g., $n \approx 0.5$ for luminance perception, $n \approx 1.5$ for electric shock).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Tip:** Fechner's Law = **logarithmic** perception (older, classical). Stevens' Power Law = **power-function** perception (modern, more accurate). For display design, Stevens' Law is preferred because it handles supra-threshold perception correctly.

---

## 2.2 Design Principles for Quantitative Displays (KTU High-Yield)

The following principles are derived from **Murrell's Hybrid Model of Human Performance** and the **Fitts–Jones Human-Engineering Guide**. They are core examination topics in KTU Module 2.

### Principle 1 — Visibility

- Display must be **within the operator's normal line of sight** (typically 0° to 30° below horizontal).
- Minimum **luminance contrast** of **3:1** for general reading.
- For critical safety displays, contrast ratio must exceed **10:1**.
- Ambient illumination must not cause glare or veiling reflections.

### Principle 2 — Legibility

- Numerals must subtend a **visual angle of at least 12 arc-minutes** at the eye.
- Stroke width of numerals ≈ 1/6 to 1/8 of numeral height.
- Standard typeface: **ISO 3476 / DIN 1451** style (avoid decorative fonts).

### Principle 3 — Comprehensibility

- Display coding must follow **population stereotypes**.
- A pointer moving to the right should indicate **increase** (Western stereotype, ≈ 95% population conformity).

### Principle 4 — Interpretability

- Scale markings must be unambiguous.
- Direction of increasing value must be obvious.
- Units must be clearly labelled.

### Principle 5 — Predictability

- Operator must be able to **forecast** what the display will do given an input change.
- A rising fuel level should move the needle up, not down.

### Principle 6 — Consistency

- Identical information across multiple displays must be presented in the **same format**.
- "Speed" on Display A and "Speed" on Display B must look the same.

### Principle 7 — Minimal Information Load

- Display only the data needed for the task.
- Avoid clutter; group related information (proximity principle).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Constants | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weber's Fraction | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | Dimensionless; $k$ = Weber constant | Determining minimum increment of scale division |
| 2 | Stevens' Power Law | $S = k \cdot I^n$ | $S$ = perceived magnitude, $I$ = stimulus | Predicting operator's subjective impression of needle deflection |
| 3 | Fechner's Law | $S = k \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{0}}\right)$ | $I_{0}$ = absolute threshold | Sizing the dynamic range of a display |
| 4 | Luminance Contrast | $C = \dfrac{\vert L_{d} - L_{b} \vert}{L_{b}}$ | $L_{d}$ = display luminance, $L_{b}$ = background luminance | Ensuring $C \geq 3:1$ for legibility |
| 5 | Visual Angle | $\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2d}\right)$ | $h$ = character height, $d$ = eye-to-display distance (arc-min) | Sizing numerals (need $\theta \geq 12$ arc-min) |
| 6 | Reading Accuracy (Analog) | $A_{a} \approx \dfrac{s}{D}$ | $s$ = scale interval width, $D$ = dial diameter | Predicting resolution of a dial |
| 7 | Reading Accuracy (Digital) | $A_{d} \approx \dfrac{1}{\text{count resolution}}$ | — | Predicting resolution of a numeric readout |
| 8 | Required Display Update Rate | $f_{u} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{signal,max}}$ | Hz (Nyquist criterion) | Avoiding flicker & aliasing |
| 9 | Snellen Acuity Reference | $V = \dfrac{d'}{d}$ | $d'$ = test distance, $d$ = standard distance (20/20 etc.) | Screening operator vision capability |
| 10 | Reaction Time (Visual) | $RT \approx 190$–$250$ ms | ms | Estimating operator loop latency |
| 11 | Speed of Reading (Digital) | $R_{d} \approx 4$–$5$ words/sec | words/sec | Comparing to analog reading |
| 12 | Speed of Reading (Analog) | $R_{a} \approx 1$–$2$ readings/sec (with precision) | readings/sec | Comparing to digital reading |
| 13 | Information Transfer (Shannon) | $H = \sum p_{i} \log_{2}(1/p_{i})$ | bits | Quantifying display information content |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Tables:** All vertical-bar symbols `|x|` in the formula sheet have been replaced with `\vert` (e.g., `\vert L_d - L_b \vert`) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do NOT use raw `|` inside table cells.

---

## 2.4 Analog vs Digital Quantitative Displays (Core Comparison)

| Parameter | Analog Display | Digital Display |
|---|---|---|
| **Reading Type** | Continuous (estimation by interpolation) | Discrete (exact digits) |
| **Reading Accuracy** | ±½ of smallest scale division | ±1 count (resolution-limited) |
| **Speed of Check Reading** | **Faster** (single glance) | Slower (must read digits) |
| **Speed of Quantitative Reading** | Slower for precise value | **Faster** for precise value |
| **Tracking Performance** | **Excellent** (continuous pointer motion) | Poor (numbers change in jumps) |
| **Direction of Change** | Easily perceived (pointer moves) | Harder to perceive (mental subtraction) |
| **Magnitude of Change** | Easily perceived (distance moved) | Harder to perceive |
| **Rate of Change** | Easily perceived (pointer speed) | Very hard to perceive |
| **Transducer Mismatch Detection** | Easy (erratic pointer motion) | Difficult |
| **Susceptibility to Vibration** | **Higher** (pointer oscillates) | Lower (digits remain stable) |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower | Higher (LED/LCD drive) |
| **Failure Mode** | Graceful (needle sticks at last value) | Often catastrophic (blank or garbled) |
| **Best Use** | Process monitoring, dynamic tracking | Precise numerical readout, log keeping |
| **Example** | Aircraft altimeter, manometer | Digital voltmeter, clock |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** *"Compare analog and digital displays and justify the choice of display type for a given engineering scenario."* — Always conclude with **task-dependent choice**, not absolute preference.

---

## 2.5 Scale Design Principles

### 2.5.1 Linear vs Non-Linear Scales

- **Linear Scale:** Equal physical spacing represents equal increments of the measured variable.
  - Best for variables that change uniformly around the operating point.
  - Easy mental arithmetic for interpolation.

- **Logarithmic Scale:** Equal physical spacing represents equal *ratios* (multiplicative increments).
  - Best for variables with very wide dynamic range (e.g., audio volume, frequency, earthquake magnitude).
  - Compresses large variations into manageable display area.

- **Square-Law / Power Scales:** Used when the operator's perception follows Stevens' Power Law with $n \neq 1$. For example, loudness perception has $n \approx 0.6$, so VU meters often use a logarithmic scale to linearize perception.

### 2.5.2 Open vs Closed Scales

- **Open Scale (Full Circle):** 360° rotation. Maximum arc length per unit value. Best for fine readings.
- **Closed Scale (270° Arc):** Standard industrial gauge. 270° of active scale, 90° blank. Good compromise.
- **Half-Open Scale (180°):** Shortest arc. Used for low-precision, compact gauges.
- **Vertical Strip / Bar Scale:** Linear, vertical. Used for level indicators (fuel, water).

### 2.5.3 Pointer Design

- **Knife-edge Pointer:** Best accuracy (operator aligns edge to scale).
- **Lance Pointer:** Tapered, used where pointer may overshoot scale.
- **Spear Pointer:** Robust, with tip and tail — allows reading from either side of pointer.
- **Recommended Pointer Length:** ≥ 80% of scale radius, but ≤ 95% (to avoid covering scale numerals).

### 2.5.4 Scale Markings

- **Major Divisions:** Numbered, thicker marks.
- **Minor Divisions:** Unnumbered, thinner marks.
- **Recommended Ratio:** Minor thickness ≈ 1/2 of major; major thickness ≈ 1/6 of scale interval width.
- **Tick Direction:** Inward-ticking scales (ticks point toward operator) are easiest to read accurately.

---

## 2.6 Information Theory Perspective

The amount of information a quantitative display conveys can be quantified using **Shannon's information theory**:

$$H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i} \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{p_{i}}\right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

For a uniform distribution of $n$ equally likely readings:

$$H = \log_{2}(n) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

For example, a 4-digit display that can show 0000–9999 carries:

$$H = \log_{2}(10000) \approx 13.29 \text{ bits}$$

A display should be designed so its information content **matches the operator's task demand**. Too many bits → cognitive overload. Too few bits → inability to detect critical changes.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Display of altitude, airspeed, vertical speed, Mach number — all are quantitatively critical for flight safety. Modern *glass cockpits* use hybrid (analog-style tapes + digital readouts) to combine the best of both worlds.
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Reactor power, coolant flow, radiation levels — quantitative displays are mandated by regulatory bodies (IAEA, AERB).
- **Automotive Safety:** Speedometer accuracy is legally mandated in most countries (typically ±10% under ECE-R39).
- **Process Industries:** Distributed Control Systems (DCS) use quantitative displays for thousands of process variables; display design follows ISA-101.01 standards.
- **Medical Devices:** Quantitative accuracy of displays on infusion pumps, ventilators, and patient monitors is life-critical (FDA IEC 60601-1-8).

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scale Interval Width for a Required Reading Accuracy

**Problem Statement:** A circular dial gauge has a dial diameter $D = 100$ mm and is required to give a reading accuracy of ±1% of full-scale value. The full-scale value is $V_{\text{fs}} = 100$ units. Determine the minimum number of scale divisions $N$ required.

**Step 1 — Express the accuracy requirement.**

$$\Delta V = \pm \, 1\% \text{ of } V_{\text{fs}} = \pm \, \dfrac{1}{100} \times 100 = \pm \, 1 \text{ unit}$$

**Step 2 — Express the relationship between accuracy and scale intervals.**

If the scale is divided into $N$ equal intervals, each interval represents a value increment of:

$$\delta V = \dfrac{V_{\text{fs}}}{N}$$

**Step 3 — The smallest resolvable reading equals ±½ of one scale interval (because the operator interpolates between marks).**

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{\delta V}{2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fs}}}{2N}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the known accuracy requirement and solve for $N$.**

$$\pm 1 = \dfrac{100}{2N}$$

$$2N = \dfrac{100}{1} = 100$$

$$N = 50 \text{ divisions}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the physical interval width on the dial.**

The circumference of the active scale arc is:

$$C_{\text{arc}} = \pi \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{\theta_{\text{arc}}}{360°}$$

For a closed-scale 270° gauge:

$$C_{\text{arc}} = \pi \times 100 \times \dfrac{270}{360} = 78.54 \text{ mm}$$

The physical interval width is:

$$s = \dfrac{C_{\text{arc}}}{N} = \dfrac{78.54}{50} = 1.57 \text{ mm per division}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification with Weber's Law:** For visual length discrimination, $k \approx 0.08$. The operator must perceive $1.57$ mm against an adjacent mark. Required minimum separation ≈ $1.57 \times 0.08 = 0.126$ mm. Since human acuity easily resolves this, the design is feasible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Numeral Height for Legibility

**Problem Statement:** A digital display is mounted on a control panel viewed from a distance of $d = 800$ mm. The display must subtend a minimum visual angle of $\theta_{\min} = 12$ arc-minutes at the operator's eye. Determine the minimum character height $h$ required.

**Step 1 — Convert arc-minutes to radians for use in trigonometry.**

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 12 \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800} = \dfrac{\pi}{900} \approx 0.003491 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the small-angle approximation formula.**

For $\theta$ small (in radians) and the display viewed from the front:

$$\theta \approx \dfrac{h}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h$.**

$$h \approx \theta_{\text{rad}} \times d = 0.003491 \times 800 = 2.79 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor for practical design (×1.5 to account for aging vision and lighting variation).**

$$h_{\text{design}} = 2.79 \times 1.5 = 4.19 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to easily fabricated size.**

$$h_{\text{design}} \approx 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A digital display viewed at 800 mm requires characters at least 4.5 mm tall. This is why automotive dashboard readouts use **15–25 mm tall characters**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Information Content of a Multi-Range Display

**Problem Statement:** An aircraft altimeter displays altitude in 20 ft increments from 0 to 50,000 ft. The pilot must discriminate the current altitude to within 20 ft for safe terrain clearance. Determine the information content of this display in bits.

**Step 1 — Determine the number of discrete possible readings.**

$$n = \dfrac{V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}}{\delta V} + 1 = \dfrac{50000 - 0}{20} + 1 = 2501 \text{ levels}$$

**Step 2 — Assume all levels are equally likely (worst-case for information theory).**

$$p_i = \dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{1}{2501}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon's formula.**

$$H = \log_{2}(n) = \log_{2}(2501)$$

$$\log_{2}(2501) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(2501)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{3.3981}{0.3010} \approx 11.29 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A trained pilot reading this altimeter must process ≈ 11.29 bits of information per glance. Compare this to human short-term memory capacity of **7 ± 2 chunks (≈ 2.8 bits per chunk)** — meaning a pilot cannot retain all the digits of a modern glass-cockpit display in short-term memory at once. This is why altimeter tape displays show the **most significant digits** with extra visual emphasis (wider tape, larger numerals).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pointer Deflection Perceived Magnitude (Stevens' Law)

**Problem Statement:** A pressure gauge pointer deflects linearly from 0° to 270° as pressure rises from 0 to 100 bar. According to Stevens' Power Law with $n = 1.2$ (visual extent on a dial), what is the perceived pressure at a pointer deflection of 200°?

**Step 1 — Express the physical stimulus in normalized form.**

$$I = \dfrac{P}{P_{\max}} = \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{\max}} = \dfrac{200°}{270°} = 0.741$$

**Step 2 — Apply Stevens' Power Law.**

$$S = k \cdot I^{n} = k \cdot (0.741)^{1.2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the power.**

$$(0.741)^{1.2} = e^{1.2 \cdot \ln(0.741)} = e^{1.2 \times (-0.3001)} = e^{-0.3602} = 0.6975$$

**Step 4 — The perceived magnitude is 69.75% of full-scale perception.**

$$S = 0.6975 \, k$$

If we set the full-scale perceived magnitude $S_{\max} = 1$ (with $I = 1$, $k = 1$), then at 200°:

$$S = 0.6975 \quad (\text{or } 69.75\%)$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to physical pressure for cross-check.**

$$P_{\text{perceived}} = S \times P_{\max} \times 100 = 0.6975 \times 100 \approx 69.75 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 6 — Bias analysis.**

The actual physical pressure at 200° is:

$$P_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{200}{270} \times 100 = 74.07 \text{ bar}$$

The *perceived* pressure (69.75 bar) **underestimates** the actual pressure by ≈ 4.32 bar. This is the **Stevens' Law compression effect**: when $n > 1$, the operator perceives high-end deflections as being *less* extreme than they physically are.

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Implication:** A high-pressure gauge with $n > 1$ may cause operators to underestimate critical high-pressure conditions. This is why critical gauges often use **expanded scales** (non-linear calibration) at the upper end.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Optimal Reading Distance for a Given Display

**Problem Statement:** A fuel-quantity indicator uses 7-segment digits 12 mm tall. What is the maximum distance at which an operator with 20/20 vision can comfortably read the display?

**Step 1 — Reference acuity threshold for comfortable reading.**

Comfortable reading visual angle: $\theta_{\text{comfort}} = 12$ arc-min.

**Step 2 — Convert to radians.**

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 12 \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800} \approx 0.003491 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the small-angle formula for distance.**

$$d_{\max} = \dfrac{h}{\theta_{\text{rad}}} = \dfrac{12}{0.003491} = 3437 \text{ mm} \approx 3.44 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply a 1.5× safety factor (typical design margin).**

$$d_{\max,\text{design}} = \dfrac{3.44}{1.5} \approx 2.29 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Result:** A 12 mm digit display is comfortably readable up to about 2.3 m. Above this distance, the operator must lean forward or the digits must be enlarged.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Display Selection Decision Matrix

| Operating Scenario | Recommended Display Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor coolant pressure in a nuclear plant | **Analog dial with expanded upper scale** | Continuous monitoring, perception of trend |
| Digital clock in a kitchen | **Digital (seven-segment LED)** | Exact time required |
| Aircraft altimeter | **Hybrid (analog tape + digital counter)** | Need trend perception AND precise readout |
| Heart-rate monitor in ICU | **Digital with analog-style bar graph** | Both precise value and trend visibility |
| Speedometer in a car | **Analog dial** | Quick check reading + trend perception |
| Voltage measurement in a lab | **Digital multimeter** | Precision quantitative reading |
| Engine RPM in a sports car | **Analog dial with red-line zone** | Tracking + warning zone demarcation |
| Inventory count in a warehouse | **Digital with barcode-driven auto-update** | Precision + automation |
| Fighter-jet attitude indicator | **Analog (artificial horizon)** | Tracking critical; digital inadequate |
| Bathroom weighing scale | **Digital LCD** | Easy reading at close range |

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Combined Accuracy & Readability Problem

**Problem:** A pressure transducer outputs 4–20 mA for 0–10 bar. The signal is conditioned and shown on a digital display with 0.00 bar resolution. The display is mounted 1.2 m from the operator.

**(a)** Calculate the **minimum character height** for legibility.
**(b)** Calculate the **minimum number of digits** required for the display.
**(c)** Calculate the **information content** of the display.

**Solution to (a):**

Using $\theta_{\min} = 12$ arc-min, $d = 1200$ mm:

$$h = \theta_{\text{rad}} \times d \times 1.5 = 0.003491 \times 1200 \times 1.5 = 6.28 \text{ mm}$$

So each digit must be **at least 6.3 mm tall**.

**Solution to (b):**

The display must show values from 0.00 to 10.00 bar. This requires **4 digits** (including decimal point): "X.XX" format.

**Solution to (c):**

The display has resolution of 0.01 bar over a range of 0–10 bar:

$$n = \dfrac{10}{0.01} + 1 = 1001 \text{ levels}$$

$$H = \log_{2}(1001) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{3.0004}{0.3010} = 9.97 \text{ bits}$$

So the display conveys **≈ 10 bits** of information per reading.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define quantitative visual displays. How do they differ from qualitative visual displays?

**Model Answer:**

Quantitative visual displays are displays that communicate the **magnitude or numerical value** of a physical variable to an operator through visual stimuli such as pointers, scales, numerical readouts, or graphs. Examples include a pressure gauge, an aircraft altimeter, or a digital voltmeter.

Qualitative visual displays, in contrast, communicate only the **status or condition** of a variable (e.g., ON/OFF, NORMAL/ABNORMAL, HEALTHY/FAIL). They do not convey magnitude. Examples include warning lamps, single-bit status indicators, and traffic-light-style status panels.

| Aspect | Quantitative Display | Qualitative Display |
|---|---|---|
| Information content | Numerical / magnitude | Status / condition |
| Reading effort | Higher (estimation or digit reading) | Lower (pattern recognition) |
| Example | Pressure gauge, altimeter | Power-on LED, warning lamp |

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for qualitative difference + 1 mark for example comparison)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** List any six design principles that govern the design of a quantitative visual display.

**Model Answer:**

The six design principles are:

1. **Visibility:** Display must be within the operator's normal line of sight with adequate contrast (≥ 3:1).
2. **Legibility:** Numerals and scale marks must subtend a visual angle of at least 12 arc-minutes at the operator's eye.
3. **Comprehensibility:** Display coding must follow population stereotypes (e.g., pointer up = increase).
4. **Interpretability:** Scale markings must be unambiguous and units must be clearly labelled.
5. **Predictability:** Operator must be able to forecast display response from input changes.
6. **Consistency:** Identical information across multiple displays must be presented in identical format.

(3 marks: ½ mark each = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q (a)** Compare analog and digital quantitative visual displays in terms of reading accuracy, reading speed, tracking performance, and failure mode. Which is more suitable for monitoring a rapidly changing process variable such as aircraft altitude during turbulence? Justify. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A circular dial gauge has a full-scale value of 200 V and a dial diameter of 150 mm. The scale is laid out over an arc of 270°. The gauge must provide a reading accuracy of ±2 V. Determine (i) the minimum number of scale divisions, (ii) the physical interval width, and (iii) the minimum character height if the scale numerals must subtend 12 arc-minutes at the operator's eye, who views the gauge from 700 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q A (a):**

| Parameter | Analog Display | Digital Display |
|---|---|---|
| **Reading Accuracy** | ±½ of smallest scale interval (interpolation-based) | ±1 count (resolution-limited) |
| **Reading Speed (check)** | Faster (single glance pattern recognition) | Slower (must read each digit) |
| **Reading Speed (precise value)** | Slower for precise value | Faster for precise value |
| **Tracking Performance** | Excellent (continuous pointer motion shows rate of change) | Poor (numbers jump discretely) |
| **Rate-of-Change Perception** | Easily perceived (pointer speed) | Hard to perceive (must mentally subtract) |
| **Failure Mode** | Graceful — needle sticks at last/failure position | Often catastrophic — blank or garbled |
| **Susceptibility to Vibration** | Higher (pointer oscillates) | Lower (digits remain stable) |

**Justification for Aircraft Altitude During Turbulence:**

Aircraft altitude during turbulence is a *rapidly changing, critical* variable. The pilot needs to:

1. **Detect small changes rapidly** — best done with a continuously moving pointer (analog tape display).
2. **Maintain awareness of rate of climb/descent** — easiest with analog needle motion.
3. **Read precise altitude to the nearest 10 or 100 ft** — provided by digital readout as a supplementary display.

**Conclusion:** A **hybrid display** (analog tape altitude with digital numeric readout, as used in modern Boeing and Airbus glass cockpits) is most suitable. If only one technology must be chosen, an **analog tape altimeter** is preferred over a purely digital one because of its superior tracking and rate-of-change perception under turbulent conditions.

(7 marks: 3 marks for comparison table + 2 marks for justification of need during turbulence + 2 marks for conclusion)

---

**Model Solution to Q A (b):**

**Given:**
- Full-scale value: $V_{\text{fs}} = 200$ V
- Dial diameter: $D = 150$ mm
- Active arc: $\theta_{\text{arc}} = 270°$
- Required accuracy: $\pm 2$ V
- Viewing distance: $d = 700$ mm
- Minimum visual angle for numerals: $\theta = 12$ arc-min

**Step (i) — Minimum number of scale divisions:**

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{\text{fs}}}{2N}$$

$$2 = \dfrac{200}{2N} \implies 2N = 100 \implies N = 50 \text{ divisions}$$

**Step (ii) — Physical interval width:**

Active arc length:

$$C_{\text{arc}} = \pi \times 150 \times \dfrac{270}{360} = 117.81 \text{ mm}$$

Width per division:

$$s = \dfrac{C_{\text{arc}}}{N} = \dfrac{117.81}{50} = 2.36 \text{ mm/division}$$

**Step (iii) — Minimum character height:**

Convert visual angle to radians:

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 12 \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800} = 0.003491 \text{ rad}$$

Apply small-angle formula:

$$h = \theta_{\text{rad}} \times d \times \text{safety factor } 1.5$$

$$h = 0.003491 \times 700 \times 1.5 = 3.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Minimum scale divisions: **50**
- (ii) Physical interval width: **2.36 mm**
- (iii) Minimum character height: **≈ 3.7 mm**

(7 marks: 2 marks for (i) + 2 marks for (ii) + 3 marks for (iii) with all intermediate steps)

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q (a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between check-reading displays and quantitative-reading displays. Discuss how the design of a quantitative display changes when the operator's primary task is **tracking** rather than **reading a precise value**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Derive the minimum number of discrete readings required for an information content of 12 bits. If a digital display uses 7-segment numerals with a height of 8 mm, what is the maximum operator-to-display distance for legible reading (assume minimum visual angle of 12 arc-minutes)? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q B (a):**

**Check-Reading Displays:**
These displays are designed to allow the operator to verify whether a variable is within an acceptable range. The reading task is *binary* (within tolerance or out of tolerance). Examples: green "SYSTEM OK" LED, "GO/NO-GO" indicator, single-bar fuel indicator.

**Quantitative-Reading Displays:**
These displays provide a precise numerical or proportional value of the variable. The reading task is *continuous*. Examples: pressure gauge reading "74.5 bar", digital thermometer reading "98.6 °F".

**Design Differences:**

| Feature | Check-Reading Display | Quantitative-Reading Display |
|---|---|---|
| Operator task | Pass/fail verification | Numerical estimation |
| Display format | Single threshold indicator (light) | Scale, pointer, or numeric readout |
| Required precision | Low (binary) | High |
| Reading time | < 1 second | Several seconds |
| Position on panel | Central, attention-grabbing | Often grouped with related variables |

**Tracking vs Precise Reading — Design Implications:**

When the primary task is **tracking** (continuous monitoring of a varying variable):

1. **Analog format is mandatory** — digits cannot show smooth trend.
2. **Pointer motion must be smooth and frictionless** — to avoid masking small changes.
3. **Scale must be linear** (or compensated for Stevens' Law).
4. **Pointer length and shape** must be designed for maximum visibility of position.
5. **Color zones on scale** help operator recognize at-a-glance status (green = safe, yellow = caution, red = danger).
6. **Display update rate** must be at least 2× the maximum signal frequency (Nyquist criterion).

When the primary task is **precise reading**:

1. **Digital format is preferred** for exact magnitude.
2. **High resolution** (more bits/digits) is required.
3. **Anti-glare & high-contrast** design is critical.
4. **Adequate illumination** under all operating conditions.

(7 marks: 2 marks for check vs quantitative distinction + 2 marks for design differences + 3 marks for tracking-specific design rules)

---

**Model Solution to Q B (b):**

**Step 1 — Number of discrete readings for $H = 12$ bits:**

Using $H = \log_{2}(n)$:

$$12 = \log_{2}(n)$$

$$n = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ discrete readings}$$

**Step 2 — Convert visual angle to radians:**

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 12 \times \dfrac{\pi}{10800} = 0.003491 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply small-angle formula for distance:**

$$d = \dfrac{h}{\theta_{\text{rad}}} = \dfrac{8}{0.003491} = 2291 \text{ mm} \approx 2.29 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 1.5× safety factor for practical design:**

$$d_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{2.29}{1.5} = 1.53 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Discrete readings needed for 12 bits: **4096**
- Maximum practical reading distance: **≈ 1.53 m**

(7 marks: 2 marks for derivation of $n$ + 2 marks for angle conversion + 3 marks for distance calculation with safety factor)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Quantitative Display Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "resolution" with "accuracy."** Resolution is the smallest increment the display can show; accuracy is the closeness of the displayed value to the true value. A display with 0.01 V resolution can still have ±0.5 V accuracy.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert arc-minutes to radians** in visual-angle calculations. Many students write $h = d \cdot \theta$ directly, forgetting that $\theta$ must be in radians for this small-angle formula.
>
> 3. **Not including the safety factor** (typically 1.5) in practical design calculations. Examiners often give 1 mark for "practical design margin."
>
> 4. **Calling analog displays "old" or "outdated."** This is factually wrong — modern glass-cockpit displays deliberately use *analog-style tapes* because they outperform purely digital displays in tracking tasks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up pointer conventions.** In Western convention, a pointer moving *clockwise* (or to the *right*) typically indicates an *increase*. In some specialized instruments (e.g., clocks), pointer direction follows convention rather than magnitude — do not assume.
>
> 6. **Ignoring failure modes.** A common question is "compare analog and digital displays" — and the *failure mode* is one of the four key comparative dimensions. Forgetting it costs 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Confusing Weber's Law with Stevens' Power Law.** Weber's Law: $\Delta I / I = k$ (just noticeable difference). Stevens' Power Law: $S = k I^n$ (perceived magnitude). Examiners *will* test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Quantitative Visual Displays**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- **Quantitative Display:** Communicates magnitude/numerical value of a variable.
- **Qualitative Display:** Communicates only status/condition.
- **Check-Reading Display:** Binary pass/fail verification.
- **Tracking Display:** Continuous monitoring of a changing variable.
- **Resolution vs Accuracy:** Smallest increment vs closeness to true value.

### Display Types

- **Analog:** Pointer + scale; excellent for tracking and rate-of-change.
- **Digital:** Numeric readout; excellent for precise quantitative reading.
- **Hybrid:** Combines both — modern aviation glass cockpit standard.

### Design Standards

- **Minimum visual angle:** 12 arc-minutes (threshold 1 arc-minute).
- **Minimum luminance contrast:** 3:1 (general), 10:1 (safety-critical).
- **Pointer direction:** Right or clockwise = increase (Western stereotype).
- **Safety factor:** 1.5× on all calculated dimensions.
- **Update rate:** ≥ 2× maximum signal frequency (Nyquist).

### Key Formulas (KTU High-Yield)

- Weber's Law: $\Delta I / I = k$
- Stevens' Power Law: $S = k \cdot I^n$
- Luminance Contrast: $C = \vert L_d - L_b \vert / L_b$
- Visual Angle: $\theta = 2 \arctan(h / 2d)$
- Information Content: $H = \log_2(n)$
- Scale Interval Width: $s = C_{\text{arc}} / N$
- Accuracy: $\Delta V = V_{\text{fs}} / (2N)$

### Comparison Traps to Avoid

- Analog is NOT obsolete — it is *superior for tracking*.
- Digital is NOT always better — it fails catastrophically on power loss.
- "Higher resolution" ≠ "higher accuracy."
- A display can be *legible* but *not comprehensible* (e.g., wrong units).

### Scenario-Based Selection Rules

| Task | Best Display |
|---|---|
| Quick status check | Analog bar / single LED |
| Precise measurement | Digital numeric |
| Continuous tracking | Analog dial |
| Both | Hybrid |
| High-vibration environment | Digital (resistant to pointer oscillation) |
| Wide dynamic range | Logarithmic scale |
| Safety-critical warning | Redundant analog + digital with audio cue |

### KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern

Always structure your 14-mark answer as:

1. **Introduction / Definition** (1–2 marks)
2. **Comparison Table or Formula Derivation** (3–4 marks)
3. **Engineering Justification for the Given Scenario** (3–4 marks)
4. **Final Recommendation with Justification** (2 marks)
5. **Conclusion / Limitations** (1 mark)

### Final Exam-Day Tip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the question says *"design a quantitative display for ___ scenario,"* always:
> 1. Identify the **task type** (check, read, track, compare).
> 2. State the **display format** you would choose and why.
> 3. Calculate the **key dimensions** (interval width, character height, dial diameter).
> 4. Mention the **failure mode** and any **redundancy** required.
> 5. Justify with **population stereotype** or **Weber/Stevens law**.

---

**END OF NOTES — QUANTITATIVE VISUAL DISPLAYS (MODULE 2, KTU 2024 SCHEME)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signals and Warning Lights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Signals and Warning Lights # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Signal (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *signal* is any perceivable stimulus (visual, auditory, tactile, or olfactory) deliberately engineered into a man-machine system to convey information regarding system state, operational status, fault condition, or impending hazard to a human operator. > [!NOTE] > **Warning Light:** A *warning light* (or visual warning signal) is a luminous indicator that uses specific color, intensity, flashing pattern, and spatial location to attract operator attention and communicate the urgency, type, and required response to abnormal conditions in industrial, vehicular, aviation, or process-control environments. **Classification Hierarchy of Signals in Human Factors Engineering:** | Class | Sub-Types | Sensory Channel | |-------|-----------|-----------------| | Visual | Lights, displays, signs, beacons, color codes | Eyes | | Auditory | Alarms, buzzers, sirens, voice messages | Ears | | Tactile | Vibrators, force feedback, pressure cues | Skin / Proprioceptors | | Olfactory | Smell additives (rare in industry) | Nose | | Gustatory | Taste cues (practically unused) | Tongue | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Traffic Signal System:** Imagine you are driving a car approaching a busy junction. Three lights (Red, Yellow, Green) glow at fixed positions. Without any spoken instruction, you instantly understand whether to stop, prepare to stop, or go. The combination of **color + position + flashing pattern** is the signal; your brain's learned response is the *perception-decision-action chain*. Now imagine a fire alarm in a hotel — the loud warbling tone instantly tells you to evacuate, even if you cannot see the smoke. This is the power of a well-designed signal: it bypasses language, culture, and conscious thought and triggers an immediate, correct response. > [!NOTE] > **Why do engineers care about signals and warning lights?** Because in critical systems (nuclear plants, aircraft cockpits, ICUs, automobiles), the **time between hazard onset and operator response** (often called *reaction time* or *response latency*) can be the difference between safety and catastrophe.
