Therefore, $10 \log_{10}(I/I_0) = 10 \log_{10}((p/p_0)^2) = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$. **Computation:** Substituting $p_{\text{rms}} = 0.05 \, \text{Pa}$: $$ \begin{aligned} L_p &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.05}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.05}{0.00002}\right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}(2500) \\ &= 20 \times 3.3979 \\ &\approx 67.96 \, \text{dB SPL} \end{aligned} $$ The machine produces approximately **68 dB SPL**, typical of light office-equipment noise. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution — Part (b):** Effective auditory warning signals must satisfy these design criteria: 1. **Frequency Selection:** Choose frequencies between **500 Hz and 4 kHz**, where the ear is most sensitive and ambient noise has less energy. 2. **Loudness:** Warning signal must be **at least 10–15 dB above ambient noise** at the operator's location to ensure detectability. 3. **Pitch Variation:** Use **alternating or warbling tones** rather than steady tones to reduce habituation and improve localization. 4. **Distinctiveness:** Each warning must be **uniquely identifiable** by its pattern, duration, and frequency (per ISO 7731). 5. **Localization:** Spatial cues help operators identify the **source of danger**. **Auditory Masking:** Masking occurs when a louder sound renders a softer, spectrally or temporally adjacent sound inaudible. In industrial settings, **high ambient noise (e.g., from machinery) can mask warning signals**, making alarms inaudible. This has led to standards like **ISO 7731**, which require alarms to differ from ambient by at least **15 dB(A)** and use distinct spectral content. Ergonomic implications include hearing-loss risk from prolonged exposure and increased accident probability due to missed warnings. *(7 marks)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Auditory Display Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing SPL with Sound Intensity Level formulas:** Students often write $L = 10 \log(p/p_0)$ instead of $20 \log(p/p_0)$. The factor is **20 for pressure**, **10 for intensity/power**. > 2. **Adding decibel values arithmetically:** Two 80 dB sources do **NOT** produce 160 dB — they produce **83 dB**. Always use $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$. > 3. **Confusing the three ossicles:** Memorize **Malleus → Incus → Stapes** (in vibration-transfer order from eardrum to oval window). > 4. **Forgetting the reference pressure value:** Always state $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ explicitly in derivations — examiners award marks for correct constants. > 5. **Confusing Place Theory with Frequency Theory:** Place Theory (von Békésy) = basilar-membrane location; Frequency Theory (Goldstein) = neural firing rate. Both apply at different frequency ranges. > 6. **Ignoring units in final numerical answers:** Always report SPL with units **dB SPL** or **dB(A)** explicitly. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Auditory Displays** use sound to convey information, especially when the operator's visual attention is occupied. - **Process of Hearing** = Outer Ear → Middle Ear → Inner Ear → Auditory Cortex (4 stages). - **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$; **Reference Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$. - **SPL Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ dB SPL. - **Most Sensitive Frequency:** 3–4 kHz (justifies alarm-tone design near this range). - **Audible Range:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz; **Pain Threshold:** ~120–140 dB SPL. - **Fletcher-Munson Curves** justify why **A-weighting** is used in noise measurement. - **Weber's Law** in audition: $\Delta I / I = k$ (just-noticeable difference). - **Inverse-Square Law:** SPL drops by **6 dB per doubling of distance** in free field. - **Combining Sources:** Use logarithmic addition, never arithmetic addition. - **Auditory Masking** dictates that alarms must exceed ambient noise by **≥ 10–15 dB**. - **Industrial Standards:** ISO 7731 (danger signals), IEC 60601-1-8 (medical alarms), OSHA 85 dB(A) limit. - **Ossicles Order:** Malleus → Incus → Stapes. - **Place Theory:** Basilar membrane encodes frequency by location. - **Critical Bands** (Bark scale) define frequency resolution of the ear (~24 bands across 20 Hz–20 kHz).

topics.title: Principles of Auditory Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Auditory Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Auditory Display** is a human–machine interface element that uses **acoustic signals** (tones, noises, speech, alarms, or complex sound patterns) to convey information, convey system status, or direct user attention in a given operational environment.

According to human factors literature (e.g., Kramer, 1994; Brewster et al.), auditory displays are classified into three primary categories:

| Category | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Alarms / Warnings** | Critical, attention-getting signals (e.g., fire alarms, ICU monitor beeps). |
| **Status Indicators** | Provide continuous information about a process (e.g., sonar pings, Geiger counter ticks). |
| **Speech Displays** | Synthesized or recorded human speech used to deliver verbal information. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Auditory displays are particularly powerful when the user's *visual channel is overloaded* or *eyes are occupied* (e.g., pilots, drivers, surgeons, control-room operators). The auditory channel is **omnidirectional** (detectable without head movement) and **immediately alerting** (the human startle reflex).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine driving a modern car:
- The **reversing beeper** alerts you to an obstacle behind the car (Warning display).
- The **turn-indicator "tick-tick"** confirms the blinker is active (Status display).
- The **voice navigation** says "Turn right in 200 meters" (Speech display).

All three are **auditory displays**. Each conveys a different *type* of information through sound. The human factors engineer's job is to **design these sounds** so they are:
1. **Detected** quickly.
2. **Discriminated** correctly from background noise.
3. **Understood** with minimum cognitive effort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:** A good auditory display is one that the user *notices immediately, interprets correctly, and finds non-annoying* over prolonged exposure. This is often called the **"3D Principle"** of auditory display design — *Detect, Discriminate, and Don't annoy*.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Reference sound pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa$ (threshold of human hearing at **1 kHz**).
- **Audible frequency range:** **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)**.
- **Speech frequency range:** **300 Hz to 3,400 Hz** (telephony band).
- **Pain threshold:** ~**120 dB SPL**.
- **Comfortable listening:** **40 dB to 60 dB SPL**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Auditory Displays (Operational Breakdown)

### A. By Information Content

1. **Simple Tonal Displays** — Single pure tones (e.g., microwave "beep").
2. **Earcons** — Abstract, structured musical tones used to represent functions in a software menu (Brewster, 1993).
3. **Auditory Icons** — Natural everyday sounds mapped to computer events (e.g., file deletion → crumpling paper sound).
4. **Spearcons** — Time-compressed speech segments used as cues.
5. **Verbal (Speech) Displays** — Synthesized or recorded voice.

### B. By Function

1. **Alerting** — Warns of a hazard or change.
2. **Identifying** — Tells *what* the event is.
3. **Status** — Indicates ongoing state (e.g., heartbeat monitor).
4. **Confirming** — Verifies user action (e.g., key-press "click").

## 2.2 Key Design Principles (Structured Logic)

### Principle 1 — Detectability
- The signal must be **louder than the masked threshold** of the environment.
- General rule: alert signals should be **at least 10–15 dB above ambient noise** (ISO 7731).
- Use multiple frequencies to overcome frequency-specific masking.

### Principle 2 — Discriminability
- Different signals must be **easily distinguished** by users.
- Use **distinct pitch contours** (rising vs. falling), **rhythms** (short-long vs. long-short), and **timbral variations**.

### Principle 3 — Learnability
- Signals should be **memorable** with minimal training.
- Auditory icons (natural sounds) score highest on learnability.
- Earcons require a learning curve but are more flexible.

### Principle 4 — Annoyance Control
- Continuous alarms cause **auditory fatigue** and are eventually ignored (the "cry-wolf effect").
- Use **intermittent, escalating alarms** instead of constant tones.
- Avoid pure tones in the 1–4 kHz range for extended durations — they fatigue quickly.

### Principle 5 — Urgency Mapping
- Higher pitch + faster pulse rate + louder volume = higher perceived urgency (Edworthy & Adams, 1996).
- This is formalized in **CAGI (Crampton's Auditory Guidance Indicator)** systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | **dB SPL** | $p_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa$ |
| **Sound Intensity Level** | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | **dB** | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \ W/m^2$ |
| **Weber's Law (Pitch JND)** | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f} = k$ | dimensionless | $k \approx 0.003$ for pitch |
| **Weber's Law (Loudness JND)** | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | dimensionless | $k \approx 0.1$ for loudness |
| **Combined Noise Levels** | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | **dB** | For incoherent sources |
| **Critical Bandwidth** | $CB \approx 25 + 75\left(1 + 1.4 f^2\right)^{0.69}$ | **Hz** | Zwicker's formula, $f$ in **kHz** |
| **Masking Threshold** | Approx. 10–15 dB above ambient | **dB** | Empirical (ISO 7731) |
| **Speech Intelligibility (SNR)** | $SNR = L_{signal} - L_{noise}$ | **dB** | SNR $\geq 6$ dB for clear speech |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Warning:** In KTU numericals, students often confuse the factor of **20** (for pressure-based) and **10** (for intensity/power-based) dB formulas. Pressure uses 20 because $I \propto p^2$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Auditory displays are critical in:
- **Aviation** — GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System), TCAS voice alerts.
- **Healthcare** — Ventilator alarms, anesthesia monitors, infusion pumps.
- **Automotive** — Lane-departure warning chimes, seat-belt reminders.
- **Industrial Control** — Boiler pressure whistles, conveyor overload sirens.
- **Consumer Electronics** — Smartphone notification sounds, smart-speaker responses.

The driving engineering rule: **When vision is overloaded, audition takes over.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Formula

We start from the definition of a **decibel (dB)**, which is a logarithmic ratio of two sound quantities.

### Step 1 — Define the Reference Pressure
The international standard reference sound pressure is the threshold of human hearing at **1 kHz**:

$$p_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa$$

### Step 2 — Form the Pressure Ratio
Take the ratio of the measured sound pressure $p$ to the reference $p_0$:

$$R = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Logarithmic Definition
The dB is defined as 20 times the base-10 logarithm of the pressure ratio (the factor 20 arises because sound intensity is proportional to $p^2$):

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(R) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Reference
Insert $p_0 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa$:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) \ \text{dB SPL}$$

> **Interpretation:** A pressure of $p = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa$ gives $L_p = 0$ dB SPL (just audible). A pressure of $p = 20 \ Pa$ gives $L_p = 120$ dB SPL (pain threshold).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combining Two Incoherent Noise Sources

When two uncorrelated (incoherent) noise sources operate simultaneously, their **sound intensities (not dB values)** add directly.

### Step 1 — Convert dB to Linear Intensities
For each source $i$, recover linear intensity using the inverse decibel formula:

$$I_i = I_0 \cdot 10^{L_i / 10}$$

where $I_0 = 10^{-12} \ W/m^2$.

### Step 2 — Sum the Linear Intensities
Total intensity is the arithmetic sum (incoherent sources):

$$I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \dots = I_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_i / 10}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Back to dB
Take 10 times the log of the total:

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_i / 10}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Special Case: Two Equal Sources
If $L_1 = L_2 = L$, then:

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(2 \cdot 10^{L/10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2) + L \approx L + 3.01 \ \text{dB}$$

> **Key Insight:** Doubling the number of identical sound sources adds only **~3 dB**, not double the loudness. To gain 10 dB (a perceived doubling of loudness), you need **10 identical sources**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weber's Law Applied to Loudness Discrimination

Weber's Law states that the **just-noticeable difference (JND)** is a constant fraction of the original stimulus.

### Step 1 — State Weber's Law

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k$$

For loudness, $k \approx 0.1$ (a 10% change in intensity is just noticeable).

### Step 2 — Express ΔI in dB
A change from $I$ to $I + \Delta I$ produces a level change $\Delta L$ in dB:

$$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I + \Delta I}{I}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1 + k)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute k = 0.1 for Loudness

$$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(1.1) = 10 \times 0.0414 \approx 0.414 \ \text{dB}$$

> **Design Rule:** To make a sound *just noticeably louder*, increase its level by at least **~0.4 dB**. To make it *perceptibly* louder, aim for **≥ 3 dB** (a doubling of acoustic power).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Masking in Design

| Masking Type | Cause | Design Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency Masking** | Loud tone hides softer tone of similar frequency. | Use frequencies separated by more than one **critical band** (~25%). |
| **Temporal Masking** | Loud sound hides softer sound shortly before/after. | Insert a **silent gap ≥ 200 ms** between signals. |
| **Cocktail-Party Effect** | Background speech masks target speech. | Use directional / spatial audio cues. |
| **Auditory Fatigue** | Continuous loud exposure raises threshold. | Use **intermittent, escalating** alarm patterns. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Auditory Display System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the engineering flow of an auditory display from the physical event to the operator's cognitive response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Process Event] --> B[Sensor / Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D{Signal Processor}
    D -->|Alarm| E1[Warning Tone Generator]
    D -->|Status| E2[Status Pulse Generator]
    D -->|Verbal| E3[Speech Synthesizer]
    E1 --> F[Audio Amplifier]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Speaker / Headphone]
    G --> H[Operator Outer Ear]
    H --> I[Cochlea Frequency Analysis]
    I --> J[Auditory Nerve Transmission]
    J --> K[Brainstem Startle Reflex]
    K --> L[Cortical Discrimination and Interpretation]
    L --> M[Operator Action or Response]
```

**Explanation of each block:**

- **A → B:** A physical event (e.g., pressure drop) is sensed.
- **C:** The raw sensor output is filtered and amplified.
- **D:** A signal processor decides *which kind* of auditory display to trigger — alarm, status, or speech.
- **E1, E2, E3:** Three different sound generators correspond to the three display categories.
- **F → G:** The signal is amplified and converted to acoustic waves.
- **H → I:** The outer ear funnels the sound; the cochlea performs a **mechanical Fourier transform**, separating frequencies.
- **J → K:** The auditory nerve fires; the brainstem mediates the **involuntary startle/orienting response**.
- **L → M:** The cortex identifies the meaning and produces a response.

---

## 4.2 Auditory Perception Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Detection Layer\nIs a sound present?] --> B[Discrimination Layer\nWhat kind of sound?]
    B --> C[Identification Layer\nWhat does it mean?]
    C --> D[Response Layer\nWhat should I do?]
```

This hierarchy reflects the **information-processing stages** of an auditory display. A well-designed display supports **all four layers** without requiring conscious effort at the lower levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** A perfectly detectable but unidentifiable alarm (e.g., all alarms sound the same) fails at the *Discrimination* and *Identification* layers — leading to user confusion and the cry-wolf phenomenon.

---

## 4.3 Earcon vs. Auditory Icon — Design Choice Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need an Auditory Cue?] --> B{Sound Source Available in Real World?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Auditory Icon\nNatural sound mapping]
    B -->|No| D{Need to Represent Abstract Data?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use Earcon\nStructured musical motif]
    D -->|No| F[Use Simple Tonal Display\nSingle beep or chime]
```

**Reading the tree:**

- If a natural everyday sound exists for the event (e.g., "trash" sound for file deletion) → use an **auditory icon**.
- If the event is abstract (e.g., a software function) → use an **earcon** (a melodic motif).
- For trivial confirmations (e.g., key-press) → use a **simple tone**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an auditory display. List any two advantages of using auditory displays over visual displays.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **auditory display** is a human–machine interface component that conveys information to the user through **acoustic signals** such as tones, noises, alarms, or synthesized speech.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Omnidirectional reception** — Sound is detected from all directions without requiring the user to look at a specific location, allowing hands-free and eyes-free monitoring (e.g., pilot warnings).
2. **Immediate alerting** — The human startle reflex causes rapid, involuntary orientation to novel sounds, making auditory displays superior for **critical warnings** that must be noticed within milliseconds.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between an earcon and an auditory icon? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Earcon | Auditory Icon |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Abstract, synthesized musical motif. | Natural, everyday recorded sound. |
| **Learnability** | Requires training. | Immediately recognizable. |
| **Example** | A two-note rising chime for "save success" in software. | A crumpling-paper sound for "file deletion." |

*(1 mark definition each + 1 mark example = 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an auditory display system**, from the physical event to the operator's response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **key design principles** of auditory displays, including detectability, discriminability, learnability, and annoyance control. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define the system (1 mark):**
An auditory display system converts a physical/operational event into a meaningful acoustic signal that an operator can detect, discriminate, identify, and respond to.

**Step 2 — Block-by-block explanation (5 marks, 1 per major block):**

1. **Sensing Stage:** Transducers (microphones, pressure sensors, temperature probes) detect the physical event.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Filters remove noise; amplifiers adjust signal levels.
3. **Decision Logic:** A microcontroller or processor decides *which kind* of audio output is required (alarm, status, or speech).
4. **Sound Generation:** Tone generators, earcon synthesizers, or speech synthesizers (TTS engines) produce the acoustic signal.
5. **Output Stage:** Amplifier + speaker (or headphone) radiates the sound into the environment.
6. **Perceptual Chain:** Outer ear → cochlea (frequency analysis) → auditory nerve → brainstem (startle reflex) → cortex (interpretation) → motor response.

**Step 3 — Closing remark (1 mark):**
The effectiveness of this system is judged not by the *hardware* quality, but by whether the operator's *response* matches the *intended message* of the designer.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Principle | Explanation | Design Recommendation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Detectability** | The signal must be heard over ambient noise. | Set alarm level **≥ 10–15 dB above ambient** (ISO 7731). |
| **Discriminability** | Different events must produce distinguishable sounds. | Use **distinct pitch, rhythm, and timbre** for each class of event. |
| **Learnability** | Users should remember sound–meaning mappings easily. | Prefer **auditory icons** (natural sounds) for frequent events. |
| **Annoyance Control** | Long exposure to loud alarms causes fatigue and habituation. | Use **intermittent, escalating** alarm patterns; avoid continuous pure tones in the 1–4 kHz band. |
| **Urgency Mapping** | Sound parameters should intuitively convey urgency. | Higher pitch, faster pulse rate, and louder volume → higher perceived urgency. |
| **Localization** | Users may need to know *where* a problem is. | Use **binaural / spatial audio** in multi-source environments. |
| **Masking Avoidance** | Strong sounds can hide weaker ones. | Separate signals by **≥ 1 critical bandwidth** and include a **silent gap ≥ 200 ms**. |

*(1 mark per correctly explained principle × 5 principles + 2 marks for design recommendations = 7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`
**(a)** Explain the **concept of masking** in auditory perception. Differentiate between **frequency masking** and **temporal masking** with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workshop has two machines producing noise levels of **78 dB** and **81 dB** respectively. Calculate the **total noise level** when both operate simultaneously. Comment on the design implications for auditory warning signals in this environment. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
**Masking** is the phenomenon in which the perception of one sound is **reduced or eliminated** by the presence of another sound. It is the auditory system's way of suppressing irrelevant acoustic information.

**Frequency Masking (2.5 marks):**
- Also called **spectral masking**.
- A loud tone at frequency $f_1$ raises the hearing threshold for nearby frequencies $f_2$ within a **critical band** (Zwicker, 1961).
- **Example:** A loud 1 kHz hum in a factory masks a 1.2 kHz alarm tone, preventing the worker from hearing it.

**Temporal Masking (2.5 marks):**
- A loud sound can mask a softer sound that occurs **shortly before (pre-masking, ~20 ms)** or **shortly after (post-masking, ~100–200 ms)** the louder sound.
- **Example:** A short click before a loud siren may go unheard because of pre-masking.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $L_1 = 78$ dB, $L_2 = 81$ dB.

**Step 1 — Write the combining formula (1 mark):**

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{78/10} + 10^{81/10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{7.8} + 10^{8.1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate linear terms (1 mark):**

$$10^{7.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{7}$$

$$10^{8.1} = 1.26 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Add and take log (2 marks):**

$$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(6.31 \times 10^{7} + 1.26 \times 10^{8}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(1.89 \times 10^{8}\right)$$

$$L_{total} = 10 \times (8.276) = 82.76 \ \text{dB}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on design implications (2 marks):**
The combined ambient level is **~82.76 dB**. To be **detectable**, any auditory warning signal in this workshop must be at least **10–15 dB above this**, i.e., **≥ 93 dB SPL**. Additionally:
- The warning frequency should be **spectrally separated** from the dominant noise frequencies of the machines to avoid frequency masking.
- The alarm should be **intermittent** (e.g., pulse on/off) to prevent auditory fatigue and to exploit temporal recovery.
- **Hearing protection** for workers must also be considered, since prolonged exposure above 85 dB causes NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Auditory Display Questions:**
> 1. **dB formula confusion:** Students write $L = 10 \log(p/p_0)$ instead of $20 \log(p/p_0)$ for pressure levels. **Always use 20 for pressure, 10 for intensity.**
> 2. **Linear addition error:** Adding dB values directly (e.g., 78 + 81 = 159) — **wrong**. dB values must first be converted to linear intensities, added, then converted back.
> 3. **Ignoring masking:** Students often recommend louder alarms without considering frequency masking. A 100 dB alarm at the same frequency as a 78 dB machine is *less detectable* than a 90 dB alarm at a separated frequency.
> 4. **Forgetting the reference value $p_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa$** in numerical problems — KTU expects the standard reference to be stated.
> 5. **Confusing earcons with auditory icons** — a classic 1-mark loss in 3-mark definition questions.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Auditory Display Definition:** A system that uses sound to convey information, particularly when vision is overloaded.
- **Three Categories:** Alarms/Warnings, Status Indicators, Speech Displays.
- **Three Sound Types:** Earcons (abstract musical motifs), Auditory Icons (natural sounds), Spearcons (compressed speech).
- **3D Design Principle:** Detect → Discriminate → Don't annoy.
- **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa$ (always state this in numericals).
- **SPL Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ in **dB SPL**.
- **Combining Noise:** $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ — convert to linear first, then add.
- **Two Equal Sources Rule:** Doubling identical sources adds **only ~3 dB**.
- **Weber's Law for Loudness:** JND ≈ **0.4 dB** (using $k = 0.1$).
- **ISO 7731 Rule:** Alarm must be **≥ 10–15 dB above ambient noise**.
- **Audible Range:** **20 Hz – 20 kHz**; Speech band: **300 Hz – 3.4 kHz**.
- **Pain Threshold:** ~**120 dB SPL**; Comfortable: **40–60 dB SPL**.
- **Masking:** Frequency masking within a critical band; temporal masking for ~200 ms after a loud sound.
- **Urgency Mapping:** Higher pitch + faster pulse + louder = higher perceived urgency.
- **Best Practice:** Prefer **auditory icons** for high-learnability cues; use **intermittent, escalating alarms** to avoid habituation.
- **Engineering Domains:** Aviation (GPWS), Healthcare (ICU monitors), Automotive (reversing beeps), Industrial control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auditory Displays for Specific Purposes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Auditory Displays for Specific Purposes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Auditory Display** is a human–machine interface component that conveys information, alerts, status, or data trends to a user through acoustic signals rather than through visual or haptic channels. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Human Factors in Engineering and Design*, **Auditory Displays for Specific Purposes** refers to the application of sound as an information-transfer medium in **safety-critical**, **task-critical**, and **data-rich environments** where the visual channel is overloaded, unavailable, or unsuitable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> *“An auditory display is any engineered sound signal — tonal, verbal, or symbolic — deliberately designed to communicate a specific piece of operational information to a human operator within a defined task context.”*

The International Standards framework supporting these displays includes:
- **ISO 7731** – Danger signals for work places
- **ISO 11428** – Auditory danger signals
- **IEC 60601-1-8** – Medical equipment alarm signals
- **ISO 24500/24501/24502** – Ergonomic auditory signals

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Timer" Model

Imagine a simple **kitchen timer** with a bell. The bell does not *show* you that the cake is done; it *announces* the condition. If you are washing dishes, looking at a child, or stirring gravy, your **eyes are busy** — but your **ears are free**. The bell exploits this spare auditory channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook:** Auditory displays for specific purposes work on the principle of *"telling the ears what the eyes cannot see right now."*

Engineering applications of this principle include:
- A **beeping pulse oximeter** alerting a nurse whose eyes are on a drug chart
- A **chime in a car** warning that the fuel is low even when the driver is looking at the road
- A **sonar ping** telling a submarine officer the depth of a target in total darkness
- A **vocalized GPS** guiding a taxi driver through an unfamiliar city at night

## 1.3 Why Auditory Displays? — The "Why" Behind the Channel

The human auditory system offers five extraordinary engineering advantages that visual displays cannot match:

| Property | Auditory Display Capability | Practical Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Omnidirectional sensing** | Sound reaches the operator from any direction | Detects alarms without line-of-sight |
| **Temporal resolution** | ~2–3 ms discrimination | Perfect for fast-changing warnings |
| **Channel independence** | Operates while eyes/hands are busy | Complements visual displays |
| **Reflexive attention capture** | Startle response within ~250 ms | Critical for danger signals |
| **Background monitoring** | Can be processed peripherally | Ideal for trend/sonar displays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The human ear can detect changes in sound **as short as 2–3 milliseconds** — a property unique to the auditory system and the reason auditory displays are mandatory in aviation cockpits, ICU monitors, and nuclear control rooms.

## 1.4 The Three Engineering Purposes

KTU 2024 categorizes auditory displays for specific purposes into **three primary functional goals**:

1. **Alerting / Warning** — Capture attention in safety-critical events (fire alarms, collision warnings).
2. **Status Monitoring / Sonification** — Convey continuous or trend data (heart monitor beeps, sonar pings, process control tones).
3. **Command / Identification** — Provide symbolic or verbal information (speech displays, earcons for menu navigation).

> [!NOTE]
> A single display may serve all three purposes simultaneously. For example, a **medical ventilator alarm** *alerts* (auditory attention capture), *monitors* (repeating beep cadence shows rate), and *identifies* (specific pitch pattern signals "high pressure" vs "low pressure").

## 1.5 The Signal Channel Hierarchy

In any engineered auditory display, sound is decomposed into four designable parameters, each mapping to a specific psychological dimension:

$$
\text{Sound} \;\longrightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{Frequency (Hz)} \rightarrow \text{Pitch perception} \\ \text{Amplitude (dB SPL)} \rightarrow \text{Loudness perception} \\ \text{Duration (ms)} \rightarrow \text{Time perception} \\ \text{Temporal Pattern} \rightarrow \text{Meaning perception} \end{cases}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** *“Frequency tells WHAT, Loudness tells HOW URGENT, Duration tells HOW LONG, Pattern tells WHO is calling.”*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Auditory Displays for Specific Purposes

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats auditory displays as a layered taxonomy. Each layer corresponds to a different engineering design philosophy.

### 2.1.1 Layer 1 — By Acoustic Content

| Display Type | Acoustic Content | Typical Use | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Tones** | Single sinusoidal frequency | Reference signals | 1 kHz test tone |
| **Complex Tones** | Multiple harmonics | Warning signals | Fire alarm bell |
| **Earcons** | Abstract musical motifs | Menu/interface feedback | Mobile phone "Marimba" |
| **Auditory Icons** | Real-world everyday sounds | Metaphorical status | File-delete "crumpling paper" |
| **Speech Displays** | Synthesized or recorded voice | Direct information | GPS navigation |
| **Sonification** | Mapped continuous sound | Data trends | Geiger counter ticking |

### 2.1.2 Layer 2 — By Functional Purpose

The International Ergonomics Association (IEA) and KTU module 2 jointly classify auditory displays for specific purposes as:

- **Warning Displays** — Emergency, Caution, Advisory levels
- **Status Displays** — Normal, Off-normal, Critical states
- **Confirmatory Displays** — Acknowledgment of user action
- **Identification Displays** — Who/what is signaling
- **Quantitative Displays** — Numerical value via sonification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students can *match* a display type to a *purpose*. The mark scheme awards credit only when both halves of the match are stated.

## 2.2 Design Variables and Their Human-Factor Mappings

Every auditory display has **four designable parameters**. Each is mapped to a specific human auditory perceptual dimension. This mapping is the heart of KTU Module 2.

### 2.2.1 Frequency (Pitch)

The pitch of an auditory signal carries **identification** information.

$$
f_{\text{usable}} \in [500,\; 3000] \;\text{Hz}
$$

**Why this range?**
- Below **500 Hz** — masked by ambient low-frequency machinery noise (HVAC, engines).
- Above **3000 Hz** — high-frequency hearing loss is most prevalent in working-age adults.
- Within this band, the ear's sensitivity is at its **maximum (threshold ~4 dB SPL at 1–2 kHz)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** Emergency warning signals MUST be within the 500–3000 Hz band, with the dominant frequency in the **1000–2000 Hz** region for optimum penetration through ambient noise.

### 2.2.2 Sound Pressure Level (Loudness)

Loudness carries **urgency** information. The engineering metric is **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** in decibels:

$$
L_p \;=\; 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{(dB SPL)}
$$

where:
- $p$ = measured RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ = reference threshold of human hearing

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often confuse **dB SPL** (absolute, referenced to 20 µPa) with **dB(A)** (A-weighted, accounting for ear sensitivity). KTU problems may give data in dB SPL — convert to dB(A) using the A-weighting curve before adding ambient noise.

**Critical Engineering Rule** (ISO 7731):

$$
L_{\text{signal}} \;\geq\; L_{\text{ambient}} + 15 \;\text{dB(A)} \quad \text{(for clear detectability)}
$$

If the signal is too quiet, it is masked. If it is more than **+25 dB(A)** above ambient, it may startle the operator and cause **auditory startle reflex** errors.

### 2.2.3 Duration (Temporal Length)

Short durations (< 200 ms) act as *attention-getters*; long durations (> 500 ms) act as *informative carriers*. The boundary is the **auditory fusion threshold** of approximately **2–3 ms** for click trains and **200 ms** for tonal integration.

### 2.2.4 Temporal Pattern

Pattern carries **identification** information. Two pulses vs three pulses vs a warble are perceptually distinct, even at identical frequency and loudness. This is the basis of the **IEC 60601-1-8** medical alarm convention.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** The following six equations account for over 80% of numerical marks in KTU Module 2 auditory display problems. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) | Convert pressure to level |
| 2 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2}dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous SPL | Long-duration average |
| 3 | $L_{\text{signal}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 15$ dB(A) | Masking threshold | Alarm detectability |
| 4 | $L_{\text{critical}} \leq 115$ dB(A) | Startle/pain limit | Maximum alarm loudness |
| 5 | $f_{\text{warning}} \in [500, 3000]$ Hz | Audibility band | Warning frequency design |
| 6 | $L_{A+B} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{L_A/10} + 10^{L_B/10})$ | Sound level addition | Combine two sources |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications — KTU Industry Mapping

KTU 2024 explicitly links the auditory display theory to **five industry sectors**:

### 2.4.1 Aviation Auditory Displays
- **Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS)** — "PULL UP" verbal command + clacker tone
- **TCAS (Traffic Collision Avoidance System)** — "CLIMB, CLIMB" verbal command
- **Master Caution/Warning** — pre-defined tonal patterns

### 2.4.2 Medical / Clinical Auditory Displays
- **IEC 60601-1-8** standard defines 8 alarm categories
- Heart monitor beep rate encodes heart rate
- Pulse oximeter pitch encodes oxygen saturation

### 2.4.3 Industrial / Process Control
- Control room sonification for nuclear, petrochemical
- Sonar displays for marine/oceanographic engineering
- Geiger counter sonification for radiological monitoring

### 2.4.4 Automotive / Driver Assistance
- Collision-avoidance beeps with urgency-graded cadence
- Lane-departure warning (directional auditory icon)
- Voice-guided navigation

### 2.4.5 Consumer / Accessibility
- Screen readers for visually impaired users
- Auditory menus for hands-busy contexts
- Mobile phone notification earcons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** KTU ESE questions in Module 2 frequently present a *case study* (e.g., "A surgeon complains of alarm fatigue in the ICU…") and ask the student to *redesign* the auditory display using the formulas above. Memorize the standards, not just the equations.

## 2.5 The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Principle in Auditory Display Design

The detectability of any auditory display in a noisy environment is governed by the **band-SNR**:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{band}} \;=\; L_{\text{signal,band}} \;-\; L_{\text{noise,band}} \quad \text{(dB)}
$$

**Masking in the same critical band:** If the noise has high energy in the same 1/3-octave band as the signal, masking is severe. Engineers therefore place warning frequencies in *quiet* bands of the ambient spectrum.

> [!TIP]
> **Design Heuristic (Bly's Method):** For every 10 dB increase in ambient noise, raise the alarm frequency by approximately **one critical band** (≈ ⅓ octave) to maintain detectability. This is a 2-mark favorite in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sound Pressure Level from a Pressure Measurement

**Problem context:** A microphone in an industrial control room measures RMS pressure of $p = 0.2$ Pa. Compute the SPL in dB and assess whether it meets the ISO 7731 emergency signal criterion, given ambient level 65 dB(A) and required minimum 15 dB(A) above ambient.

### Step 1 — Identify the reference quantity
The reference pressure for human hearing in air is:
$$
p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{Pa}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the SPL formula
$$
L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)
$$
$$
L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the ratio
$$
\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10^{4}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm
$$
L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}(10^{4}) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \;\text{dB SPL}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply ISO 7731 detectability criterion
The required signal level is:
$$
L_{\text{required}} = L_{\text{ambient}} + 15 = 65 + 15 = 80 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare and conclude
The measured signal is **80 dB SPL**, and assuming the spectrum falls in the 1–2 kHz band, the A-weighted value is approximately equal to SPL (A-weighting correction ≈ 0 dB at 1 kHz).
$$
L_{\text{signal}} = 80 \;\text{dB(A)} \;\geq\; 80 \;\text{dB(A)} = L_{\text{required}} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The signal just satisfies the minimum criterion. For a **safety margin**, the engineer should design for at least +18 dB(A) above ambient to compensate for **operator hearing variability** and **PPE attenuation** (earplugs typically give 20–30 dB attenuation, but alarms are designed assuming no PPE).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combining Two Co-located Auditory Sources

**Problem context:** A control room has two signal speakers. Speaker A emits 78 dB SPL; Speaker B emits 78 dB SPL at the operator's ear. Find the **total** SPL.

### Step 1 — State the incoherent source addition rule
When two sound sources are uncorrelated, intensities add, not levels:
$$
L_{A+B} = 10 \, \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_A/10} + 10^{L_B/10}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values
$$
L_{A+B} = 10 \, \log_{10}\!\left(10^{78/10} + 10^{78/10}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Factor the common term
$$
L_{A+B} = 10 \, \log_{10}\!\left(2 \times 10^{7.8}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply logarithm identity $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$
$$
L_{A+B} = 10 \left[\log_{10}(2) + 7.8\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate
$$
\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010
$$
$$
L_{A+B} = 10 (0.3010 + 7.8) = 10 \times 8.1010 = 81.01 \;\text{dB SPL}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation
Doubling identical sources raises the level by only **+3 dB** (the famous *"3 dB doubling rule"*). This is critical in alarm design: **adding more speakers does NOT solve masking — it only buys 3 dB per doubling**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The "3 dB Doubling" General Rule

**Generalization:** For $N$ identical, uncorrelated sound sources, each at level $L$:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L + 10 \log_{10}(N) \quad \text{(dB)}
$$

**Step 1:** For $N = 2$:
$$
\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(2) = 3.01 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 2:** For $N = 10$:
$$
\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3:** For $N = 100$ (a 100-speaker array, theoretical):
$$
\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \;\text{dB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "+3 dB per doubling" without showing the formula. The mark scheme requires the formula and the substitution.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Auditory Display Type

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to *justify* the choice of auditory display type for a given purpose. The following matrix is the official mapping used in the textbook approach:

| Purpose | Recommended Display | Justification | Failure Mode If Ignored |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fire emergency | Tonal warble (500–3000 Hz) + verbal "EVACUATE" | Maximum detectability, ISO 7731 | Localization errors if broadband noise used |
| ICU medical alarm | Pulsed tone (IEC 60601-1-8 melody) | Distinguishable from background beeps | Alarm fatigue if too many similar tones |
| Sonar / process | Periodic pulse (ping) | Encodes rate-of-change | Loss of trend information |
| Vehicle reverse | Auditory icon (beep-beep-beep) | Maps to natural "warning" semantics | Driver ignores abstract earcon |
| GPS navigation | Speech display | Direct unambiguous message | Memory load if non-speech used |
| Data trend sonification | Continuous parameter mapping | Leverages auditory resolution of change | Operator cannot perceive small variations |
| Menu navigation | Earcons (musical motif) | Compact, distinguishable | Confused with other interface sounds |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — The KTU-Style 14-Mark Style

**Problem:** A factory floor has ambient A-weighted SPL of **80 dB(A)**. An emergency stop signal must be at least **+15 dB(A)** above ambient. The available alarm generator can produce a tone of either **700 Hz** or **2500 Hz**. Spectral analysis shows the ambient noise has dominant energy at **800 Hz** with a 1/3-octave band level of **82 dB(A)**, and at **200 Hz** with **75 dB(A)**.

**Task:** Recommend the warning frequency and the minimum required signal level at the operator's ear.

### Step 1 — Calculate the required signal level
$$
L_{\text{req}} = L_{\text{ambient,broadband}} + 15 = 80 + 15 = 95 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the band-SNR masking principle
The 700 Hz alarm falls in the 1/3-octave band around 800 Hz, which has **82 dB(A)** of masking energy. To overcome this:
$$
L_{\text{signal,700}} = 82 + 15 = 97 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

The 2500 Hz alarm falls in a 1/3-octave band where the ambient is much lower (assume 70 dB(A) by spectral roll-off):
$$
L_{\text{signal,2500}} = 70 + 15 = 85 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare
- 700 Hz: needs 97 dB(A) — close to the startle limit of 115 dB(A)
- 2500 Hz: needs 85 dB(A) — comfortably safe

### Step 4 — Decision and justification
**Choose 2500 Hz** because:
1. It lies in a *quieter* critical band of the ambient spectrum.
2. It requires less acoustic power, reducing hearing-damage risk.
3. It is within the ISO-recommended warning band (500–3000 Hz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer for KTU Mark Scheme:**
> Recommended frequency = **2500 Hz**
> Minimum signal level = **95 dB(A)** (broadband criterion) and **85 dB(A)** (band-masking criterion, governing).

## 3.6 The Equal-Loudness Contour Principle (Fletcher-Munson Curves)

The human ear is not equally sensitive at all frequencies. At low SPL, the ear emphasizes mid-frequencies (1–4 kHz); at high SPL, the response flattens. Auditory display engineers must add the **A-weighting correction** when comparing signal and noise in dB(A).

The A-weighting correction at 1 kHz is defined as **0 dB** by definition. Approximate values for engineering design:

| Frequency (Hz) | A-weighting correction (dB) |
|---|---|
| 125 | −16.1 |
| 250 | −8.6 |
| 500 | −3.2 |
| 1000 | 0.0 |
| 2000 | +1.2 |
| 4000 | +1.0 |
| 8000 | −1.1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A 100 dB SPL tone at 125 Hz is only **83.9 dB(A)** at the ear. This is why low-frequency alarms are easily masked by speech and must be louder to be detected.

## 3.7 Masking Bandwidth Analysis

**Critical Band** is the ear's frequency resolution window. Within a critical band, one sound can mask another.

The critical bandwidth (in Hz) is approximated by:
$$
\text{CB} \approx 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4 f^2\right)^{0.69} \quad \text{(Hz)}
$$

For $f = 1000$ Hz:
$$
\text{CB} = 25 + 75 (1 + 1400)^{0.69} \approx 25 + 75 \times 76 \approx 160 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 1000 Hz warning can be masked by any noise energy between roughly **920 Hz and 1080 Hz**. Designers therefore place alarms in *gaps* of the ambient spectrum, not in its peaks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Auditory Display System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the engineering flow of a generic auditory display for specific purposes — from the data source through the human operator. This is the architecture the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SOURCE DATA] --> B[TRANSDUCER AND SENSOR]
    B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING UNIT]
    C --> D[ACOUSTIC PARAMETER MAPPER]
    D --> E[SOUND GENERATOR TONE VOICE EARCON]
    E --> F[AMPLIFIER AND ATTENUATOR]
    F --> G[ACOUSTIC CHANNEL AIR PANEL SPEAKER]
    G --> H[OPERATOR EAR PINNA COCHLEA]
    H --> I[PERCEPTION AND DECISION]
    I --> J[RESPONSE ACTION]

    C -.ambient noise.-> G
    I -.feedback loop.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every block corresponds to a design decision the engineer must make. For example, the **ACOUSTIC PARAMETER MAPPER** is where the mapping "heart rate → pitch" or "fuel level → pulse rate" is defined. KTU 14-mark questions frequently ask students to *redesign* this mapper.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Auditory Display Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[TASK PURPOSE] --> Q{IS EYE CHANNEL BUSY?}
    Q -- YES --> R[USE AUDITORY DISPLAY]
    Q -- NO --> S[CONSIDER VISUAL DISPLAY FIRST]

    R --> T{WHAT IS THE PURPOSE?}
    T -- URGENT WARNING --> U[TONAL WARBLE OR VERBAL]
    T -- TREND OR DATA --> V[SONIFICATION PARAMETER MAPPING]
    T -- IDENTIFICATION --> W[EARCON OR AUDITORY ICON]
    T -- DIRECT MESSAGE --> X[SPEECH DISPLAY]

    U --> Y{AMBIENT NOISE LEVEL}
    V --> Y
    W --> Y
    X --> Y

    Y -- LESS THAN 65 DBA --> Z[FREQUENCY 500 TO 3000 HZ]
    Y -- GREATER THAN 80 DBA --> AA[USE HIGHER FREQUENCY GAP BAND]
    Y -- GREATER THAN 95 DBA --> AB[ADD VIBRATORY TACTILE BACKUP]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this flowchart earns full marks:** Examiners award credit for showing the *decision logic* (branching), not just the answer. The conditional nodes `Q`, `T`, `Y` map directly to the **Purpose → Channel → Parameter → Environment** decision sequence taught in Module 2.

## 4.3 Layered Taxonomy of Auditory Displays

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[AUDITORY DISPLAYS FOR SPECIFIC PURPOSES]

    ROOT --> P1[BY ACOUSTIC CONTENT]
    ROOT --> P2[BY FUNCTIONAL PURPOSE]
    ROOT --> P3[BY APPLICATION DOMAIN]

    P1 --> C1[PURE TONES]
    P1 --> C2[COMPLEX TONES]
    P1 --> C3[EARCONS]
    P1 --> C4[AUDITORY ICONS]
    P1 --> C5[SPEECH]
    P1 --> C6[SONIFICATION]

    P2 --> F1[ALERTING AND WARNING]
    P2 --> F2[STATUS AND MONITORING]
    P2 --> F3[CONFIRMATORY]
    P2 --> F4[IDENTIFICATION]
    P2 --> F5[QUANTITATIVE]

    P3 --> A1[AVIATION GPWS TCAS]
    P3 --> A2[MEDICAL IEC 60601]
    P3 --> A3[INDUSTRIAL CONTROL]
    P3 --> A4[TRANSPORT VEHICLE]
    P3 --> A5[CONSUMER ACCESSIBILITY]
```

## 4.4 The Operator Decision Loop with Auditory Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[OPERATOR] -->|DIRECTS ATTENTION| O2[PRIMARY VISUAL TASK]
    O1 -->|PERIPHERAL LISTENING| O3[AUDITORY DISPLAY]
    O3 -->|AUDITORY ALERT| O1
    O2 -->|COMPLETED OR INTERRUPTED| O4[RESPONSE]
    O1 --> O4
    O4 -->|FEEDBACK| O3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** This is the *dual-task* model. The auditory display functions as a **peripheral watcher** that only breaks into the conscious attention loop when the signal exceeds a pre-set salience threshold. This is the engineering basis of alarm design.

## 4.5 Parameter-to-Meaning Mapping Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FQ[FREQUENCY HZ] --> M1[IDENTIFICATION WHAT IS IT]
    AM[AMPLITUDE DBA] --> M2[URGENCY HOW CRITICAL]
    DR[DURATION MS] --> M3[RELEVANCE HOW LONG]
    PT[PATTERN PULSES WARBLE] --> M4[SOURCE WHO IS CALLING]

    M1 --> OUT[OPERATOR PERCEIVED MEANING]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule (Brewster, 1998; adopted by KTU Module 2):** "Each designable acoustic parameter should carry **one and only one** semantic meaning. Mixed mappings are the leading cause of operator confusion in alarm design."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*"Define an auditory display. List any three functional purposes of auditory displays in engineering systems."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **auditory display** is an engineered acoustic signal designed to communicate specific operational information to a human operator through the sense of hearing (1 mark). The three functional purposes are:
1. **Alerting / Warning** — capturing attention for safety-critical events (1 mark).
2. **Status Monitoring** — conveying continuous or trend information such as heart rate or sonar depth (0.5 mark).
3. **Identification / Command** — providing symbolic, earcon, or verbal information to identify system states (0.5 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Marks are split across *definition (1)* + *three purposes (2)*. A bare list with no definition scores maximum 2 marks.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**
*"State the ISO 7731 detectability criterion for an emergency auditory signal. Why is +15 dB(A) chosen as the minimum margin?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ISO 7731 criterion states:
$$
L_{\text{signal}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 15 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$
The **+15 dB(A) margin** accounts for: (i) operator's reduced sensitivity in noisy environments, (ii) hearing-protector attenuation variability, and (iii) the masking of the signal by ambient peaks (1 mark each). Below 15 dB(A), signal detectability drops below 95% in laboratory tests; above 25 dB(A), the startle reflex impairs correct response (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A: Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2]**

> *"Auditory displays are a critical component of modern human–machine systems. Answer the following in the context of a hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)."*
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** Discuss the design considerations for auditory alarm displays in an ICU under the **IEC 60601-1-8** standard. Cover frequency, loudness, duration, and pattern in your answer.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** The ambient noise level in the ICU is **62 dB(A)** during the day and **48 dB(A)** at night. The current alarm sound pressure level is **72 dB SPL** at the bedside. Determine whether the alarm meets the ISO 7731 detectability criterion under both conditions, and recommend corrective action if it does not.

### Solution B-A (a) — Design Considerations Under IEC 60601-1-8 (7 marks)

**1. Frequency Design (2 marks)**
The standard mandates **8 alarm categories**, each with a specific melodic pattern of harmonically-related tones in the **150–1000 Hz** range. The dominant frequencies are chosen to be *distinguishable from the speech band* to avoid being masked by conversation.

**2. Loudness Design (2 marks)**
The minimum sound pressure level shall be at least **+10 dB(A)** above ambient, with a recommended level of **+15 dB(A)**. The maximum level must not exceed **80 dB(A)** at 1 m to avoid hearing damage to patients and staff (startle limit).

**3. Duration Design (1.5 marks)**
Pulse durations of **125–250 ms** with 50% duty cycle. Total alarm burst must repeat at least **4 times** with inter-burst intervals of 2.5–30 s to ensure recognition.

**4. Pattern Design (1.5 marks)**
Each alarm category has a unique melodic pattern (e.g., "Cardiac" — single high-low tone; "Respiration" — three rising tones). This pattern-based coding allows the operator to *identify* the alarm source without reading the screen.

### Solution B-A (b) — Numerical Detectability Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Daytime analysis**
$$
L_{\text{req,day}} = L_{\text{ambient,day}} + 15 = 62 + 15 = 77 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$
The bedside signal is 72 dB SPL. Assuming the dominant frequency lies in the 500–2000 Hz range, the A-weighted level is approximately **72 dB(A)**.
$$
L_{\text{signal}} = 72 \;\text{dB(A)} \;<\; 77 \;\text{dB(A)} = L_{\text{req,day}} \quad \times
$$
**Daytime criterion FAILED by 5 dB(A).**

**Step 2 — Nighttime analysis**
$$
L_{\text{req,night}} = 48 + 15 = 63 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$
$$
L_{\text{signal}} = 72 \;\text{dB(A)} \;\geq\; 63 \;\text{dB(A)} \quad \checkmark
$$
**Nighttime criterion PASSED** (with +9 dB margin).

**Step 3 — Recommended Corrective Action (2 marks)**
- **Increase the alarm SPL to at least 77 dB(A)** during the day by using a directional speaker aimed at the assigned nurse.
- **Add a vibratory tactile alarm** at the bedside to provide a *secondary* channel for the patient-side and reduce reliance on the over-burdened auditory channel (combatting *alarm fatigue*).
- **Re-evaluate the alarm category set** to reduce the total alarm count by 30% (best practice from Joint Commission 2014).

---

### Question B-B: Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Retest]**

> *"The cockpit of a regional airliner is being retrofitted with a new auditory Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS)."*
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** Explain why auditory displays are preferred over visual displays for the GPWS in this application, and design the acoustic parameters of the warning signal (frequency, level, duration, pattern).
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** The cockpit ambient noise is **75 dB(A)**, and the new warning is specified to produce **95 dB SPL** at the pilot's ear. The signal is at 2500 Hz. Determine: (i) the A-weighted signal level, (ii) the SNR in dB(A), (iii) whether the criterion is met, and (iv) what action the engineer should take if the pilot uses **20 dB attenuation** earplugs.

### Solution B-B (a) — Design Justification and Parameter Specification (7 marks)

**Why Auditory over Visual (3 marks):**
1. The pilot's eyes are fixated outside the cockpit (VFR) or on primary flight displays (IFR) — visual channel is **saturated**.
2. Auditory warnings have a **startle response time of ~250 ms** vs ~500 ms for visual change detection — critical for terrain avoidance.
3. Auditory signals are **omnidirectional** — pilot does not need to be looking at a specific instrument.
4. Industry mandate: FAA TSO-C151a and ICAO Annex 6 require auditory GPWS.

**Acoustic Parameter Design (4 marks):**
| Parameter | Designed Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 2500 Hz | Avoids masking by engine and wind noise (peaks < 1000 Hz) |
| Level | 95 dB SPL at ear | +20 dB above 75 dB(A) ambient; below 115 dB startle limit |
| Duration | 250 ms pulse, 50% duty | Long enough to integrate, short enough to repeat |
| Pattern | 3 pulses + 1 long (clacker) | ICAO standardized "PULL UP" precursor pattern |

### Solution B-B (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert 95 dB SPL at 2500 Hz to dB(A) (2 marks)**
From the A-weighting table (Section 3.6), the correction at 2000 Hz is **+1.2 dB**, at 4000 Hz is **+1.0 dB**. Linear interpolation at 2500 Hz:
$$
\Delta L_A \approx +1.15 \;\text{dB}
$$
$$
L_{\text{signal,A}} = 95 + 1.15 = 96.15 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SNR in dB(A) (1 mark)**
$$
\text{SNR} = L_{\text{signal,A}} - L_{\text{ambient,A}} = 96.15 - 75 = 21.15 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$

**Step 3 — ISO 7731 compliance (1 mark)**
$$
L_{\text{req}} = 75 + 15 = 90 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$
Since 96.15 dB(A) ≥ 90 dB(A), the criterion is **met** with a +6.15 dB margin.

**Step 4 — Effect of 20 dB earplugs (3 marks)**
The earplug attenuates both signal and ambient by 20 dB.
$$
L_{\text{signal,protected}} = 96.15 - 20 = 76.15 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$
$$
L_{\text{ambient,protected}} = 75 - 20 = 55 \;\text{dB(A)}
$$
$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{protected}} = 76.15 - 55 = 21.15 \;\text{dB(A)} \quad \text{(unchanged)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Earplugs reduce *absolute* levels but **do not change SNR** because they attenuate signal and noise equally. Therefore the detectability criterion is **still met**, and the design is robust against standard PPE. However, the engineer should verify the *absolute* protected level exceeds the **auditory threshold in the cockpit** (typically 25 dB(A) for an alert pilot) — here 76.15 dB(A) is well above, so the design is safe.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical):**
> - Students commonly **forget the A-weighting correction** in Step 1 and lose 1 mark. Always interpolate the A-weighting table for non-tabulated frequencies.
> - Step 4 marks are awarded only if the student **explains** why SNR is preserved (attenuation applies equally to signal and noise), not just numbers.
> - The question deliberately tests *PPE interaction with auditory displays* — a Module 2 learning outcome. Marks are reserved for the discussion of threshold vs SNR distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Module 2 Revision Block — Auditory Displays for Specific Purposes**

- [x] **Definition:** Auditory displays communicate information through engineered sound in safety-critical, task-critical, or data-rich environments.
- [x] **Three Purposes:** Alerting, Status Monitoring, Command/Identification.
- [x] **Four Designable Parameters:** Frequency (identification), Amplitude (urgency), Duration (relevance), Pattern (source).
- [x] **Warning Frequency Band:** $f \in [500, 3000]$ Hz, with dominant energy in 1000–2000 Hz.
- [x] **ISO 7731 Detectability:** $L_{\text{signal}} \geq L_{\text{ambient}} + 15$ dB(A).
- [x] **Startle Limit:** $L_{\text{critical}} \leq 115$ dB(A); ICU limit 80 dB(A) at 1 m.
- [x] **3 dB Doubling Rule:** Doubling identical sound sources adds only +3 dB; 10 sources add +10 dB; 100 sources add +20 dB.
- [x] **SPL Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ where $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.
- [x] **A-weighting:** 1 kHz = 0 dB reference; 125 Hz = −16.1 dB; 4000 Hz = +1.0 dB.
- [x] **IEC 60601-1-8:** 8 alarm categories with distinct melodic patterns; 150–1000 Hz range; pulse 125–250 ms.
- [x] **Earcons vs Auditory Icons:** Earcons = abstract musical motifs; Auditory Icons = real-world sounds mapped metaphorically.
- [x] **Sonification:** Continuous parameter mapping (heart rate → pitch) for trend monitoring.
- [x] **Speech Displays:** Used when message is unambiguous and time allows; e.g., GPS, GPWS "PULL UP".
- [x] **PPE Interaction:** Earplugs reduce both signal and ambient by the same amount, leaving SNR unchanged — but absolute protected level must still exceed threshold.
- [x] **Alarm Fatigue Mitigation:** Reduce alarm count by 30%, use distinct patterns, add tactile backup, escalate severity grading.
- [x] **Critical Band Masking:** A signal is masked by noise in the same 1/3-octave band; place alarms in *gaps* of the ambient spectrum.
- [x] **Bly's Heuristic:** Raise alarm frequency by ~1 critical band per 10 dB of ambient noise increase.
- [x] **Design Principle (Brewster 1998):** Each acoustic parameter must carry **one and only one** semantic meaning.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** *"Frequency says WHAT, Amplitude says HOW URGENT, Duration says HOW LONG, Pattern says WHO — and ISO 7731 says +15 dB(A)."* This single sentence covers ~40% of the KTU Module 2 mark scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compatibility - Types of Compatibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compatibility - Types of Compatibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)
> **Compatibility** is defined as the degree to which the *characteristics* of a *system*, *product*, *environment*, or *interface* match the *characteristics*, *capabilities*, *expectations*, or *limitations* of the *user* (operator). High compatibility reduces learning time, minimizes errors, lowers mental workload, and improves overall human-machine system performance.

In the context of **Human Factors and Ergonomics**, compatibility is fundamentally a measure of the **mismatch (or fit) between the human and the machine**. The greater the compatibility, the more *natural, intuitive, and error-free* the human-machine interaction becomes.

> [!NOTE] KTU Module 2 Context
> In Module 2, compatibility is treated as a fundamental *design principle* that governs how displays, controls, workspaces, and information architectures are engineered to match human perceptual, cognitive, and motor characteristics. It is one of the cornerstones of *user-centered design* and *human-machine integration*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world Analogy — The "Foreign Remote Control" Problem**

Imagine you buy a new television remote control. If pressing the **right** arrow button moves the menu selection to the **right** on the screen, you are using it intuitively — almost without thinking. This is **high spatial compatibility**. The spatial arrangement of your finger movement matches the spatial movement of the on-screen cursor.

Now imagine the opposite: the **right** arrow moves the menu **left**, and the **up** arrow selects the current option instead of moving up. You would need to consciously think about *every* button press, get frustrated quickly, and make frequent errors. This is **low compatibility** — the system's logic contradicts your brain's natural expectations.

> [!TIP] The 30-Second Mental Model
> **Compatibility = "Friction" between Human Intuition and System Logic**
> - **High compatibility** → Low friction → Fast, accurate, low-effort operation
> - **Low compatibility** → High friction → Slow, error-prone, high mental workload

Another familiar example: in **automatic cars**, pushing the gear lever **forward** puts the car in **Drive (D)** and pulling it **back** puts it in **Reverse (R)**. This is a *population stereotype* — almost everyone globally expects this. If a manufacturer reversed it, accidents would be inevitable.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Compatibility

Although compatibility is often qualitative, it is empirically measured using:

- **Response Time (RT)** measured in **milliseconds (ms)** — Shorter RT implies higher compatibility.
- **Error Rate** (errors per 1000 operations) — Lower error rate implies higher compatibility.
- **NASA-TLX Score** (0–100 scale) — Lower workload implies higher compatibility.
- **Transfer-of-Training Time** measured in **hours** — Less retraining time implies higher compatibility.
- **Signal Detection Metrics** such as $d'$ (sensitivity index) and **hit rate / false-alarm rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT] Key Engineering Insight
> Compatibility is **not a binary** property; it exists on a continuous spectrum. Designers must **maximize** compatibility on this spectrum to achieve *operational excellence* and *safety-critical reliability*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Compatibility Hierarchy

Compatibility in human factors is organized into a **hierarchical taxonomy**. The most widely accepted framework (per *Wickens, Lee, Liu, & Gordon-Becker — Introduction to Human Factors Engineering*) classifies compatibility into the following types:

### 2.1.1 Stimulus–Response (S–R) Compatibility

This refers to the **mapping between a stimulus (input / display) and a response (output / control)** that the user must produce. S–R compatibility is sub-divided into:

#### (a) Population Stereotype Compatibility
- Based on **learned cultural expectations** shared by a population.
- Example: **Red = Stop**, **Green = Go**; turning a steering wheel **clockwise** = turn **right**; **Up arrow = increase**.
- Violations cause **systematic, predictable errors** because the population's stereotype is violated.

#### (b) Spatial Compatibility
- Based on the **physical / spatial correspondence** between stimulus and response locations.
- Example: A **left-hand joystick** moves a vehicle **left**; a **red warning light** is paired with a **red emergency button** located nearby.
- High spatial compatibility reduces *visual scanning time* and *cross-referencing effort*.

#### (c) Movement Compatibility
- Based on the **kinematic / directional correspondence** between the operator's limb movement and the system's response direction.
- Example: Pushing the control **stick forward** makes the aircraft **pitch down** (or, in some helicopters, **climb** — a known compatibility violation).
- Also called **"control–display compatibility"** in instrumentation design.

---

### 2.1.2 Person–Machine (P–M) Compatibility

This refers to the broader **fit between the human's perceptual, cognitive, and physical characteristics and the machine's interface design**. It includes:

- **Perceptual compatibility** — Display legibility, color coding, contrast, symbol recognition.
- **Cognitive compatibility** — Mental model match, information chunking, decision-support logic.
- **Physical/Anthropometric compatibility** — Control sizes, reach distances, force requirements matched to human body dimensions.

> [!NOTE] Why It Matters
> Person–machine compatibility is the *holistic* category that integrates anthropometry, biomechanics, sensation, perception, and cognition. It is what a designer tunes when they say, "I made it ergonomic."

---

### 2.1.3 Conceptual Compatibility

- Based on the **match between the user's mental model** (internal representation) **and the system's actual operational model**.
- When conceptual compatibility is high, the user can **predict, anticipate, and debug** system behavior intuitively.
- Example: A file system's *folder* metaphor in a GUI leverages conceptual compatibility with the physical "office folder" mental model.

---

### 2.1.4 Cultural Compatibility

- A specialized form of population stereotype that varies **across nations, regions, or social groups**.
- Example: **Color semantics differ** — in Western cultures, *white* symbolizes *purity / weddings*; in parts of East Asia, *white* symbolizes *mourning*. A bridal-shop website using white for "Out of Stock" would violate cultural compatibility in China.

---

### 2.1.5 Sensory / Modality Compatibility

- The match between the **modality of the stimulus** (visual, auditory, tactile) and the **human sensory channel** best suited to detect it under the given conditions.
- Example: A **loud auditory warning** in a high-noise factory is more compatible than a visual warning that the operator may not be facing.
- Aligned with **Multiple Resource Theory** (Wickens, 2008).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Meaning | Units | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
|---|---------|-----------------------|---------------------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Fitts' Law (Movement Compatibility) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( 1 + \dfrac{D}{W} \right)$ | $MT$ = movement time, $D$ = distance to target, $W$ = target width, $a,b$ = empirically fitted constants | $MT$ in **seconds (s)**, $D$ and $W$ in **meters (m)** or **pixels** | Valid for **discrete aimed movements**; degrades for trajectories |
| 2 | Index of Difficulty (ID) | $ID = \log_2 \!\left( 1 + \dfrac{D}{W} \right)$ | $ID$ = bits of information processed | **bits** | $ID \geq 0$ always |
| 3 | Signal Detection Sensitivity | $d' = Z(H) - Z(F)$ | $Z(H)$ = z-score of hit rate, $Z(F)$ = z-score of false-alarm rate | dimensionless | Higher $d'$ = better perceptual compatibility |
| 4 | Hick's Law (Choice Compatibility) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $T$ = reaction time, $N$ = number of equally probable choices | $T$ in **seconds (s)**, $N$ dimensionless | Applies when $N \leq 10$ |
| 5 | Compatibility Error Rate (Empirical) | $E_r = \dfrac{N_{err}}{N_{trials}} \times 100$ | $N_{err}$ = errors, $N_{trials}$ = total trials | **percentage (%)** | Lower $E_r$ = higher compatibility |
| 6 | Mean Response Time (RT) | $\overline{RT} = \dfrac{1}{n} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} RT_i$ | Average response time across $n$ trials | **milliseconds (ms)** | Used to compare compatibility conditions |
| 7 | Workload Reduction via Compatibility | $\Delta W = W_{incompat} - W_{compat}$ | Reduction in workload (e.g., NASA-TLX) | **points (0–100 scale)** | Positive $\Delta W$ = net benefit |
| 8 | Force Compatibility Limit | $F_{op} \in [F_{min}, F_{max}]$ | $F_{op}$ must lie within 5th–95th percentile strength | **Newtons (N)** | Per **NIOSH** and **MIL-STD-1472** guidelines |

> [!TIP] Examiner's Note
> For KTU Module 2 problems, **Fitts' Law** and **Hick's Law** are the most frequently tested equations. Memorize their variable definitions and units — most mark loss occurs from incorrect unit declarations.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Compatibility Principle |
|--------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Avionics & Cockpit Design** | Control–display mapping, color-coded warnings, spatial arrangement of instruments |
| **Automotive HMI** | Steering wheel direction ↔ wheel rotation (movement compatibility); gear-shift conventions (population stereotype) |
| **Industrial Control Rooms** | Annunciator panels arranged to mirror plant geography (spatial compatibility) |
| **Medical Device Design** | Infusion pump button logic ↔ nurse mental model (conceptual compatibility) |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Smartphone gesture vocabulary (swipe up = scroll up) — movement + spatial |
| **Nuclear Power Plants** | Sensor modality selection (visual + auditory redundancy) — sensory compatibility |
| **Web & App UX Design** | Hamburger menu icon, trash icon for delete (conceptual compatibility) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fitts' Law (Movement Compatibility)

Fitts' Law is the foundational equation of **movement compatibility** and is highly likely to be asked in KTU derivations. The full derivation proceeds from **information theory**.

### Step 1 — Define the Task as an Information-Theoretic Channel

Paul Fitts (1954) modeled a rapid aimed movement as a **noisy communication channel** through which the human motor system transmits information about the target's location.

The **index of difficulty (ID)** of the task is defined as the amount of information (in bits) required to identify the target unambiguously.

### Step 2 — Express the Target's Spatial Uncertainty

Consider a target of width $W$ located at distance $D$ from the starting position. The movement amplitude $A = D$ and the target tolerance is $T = W$.

The **distance moved relative to the target size** is:

$$
\dfrac{A}{T} = \dfrac{D}{W}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Shannon's Channel Capacity Formula

By analogy with Shannon's logarithmic measure of information:

$$
ID = \log_2 \!\left( \dfrac{A}{T} \right)
$$

To ensure $ID \geq 0$ (since $A \geq T$ in nearly all aimed tasks), Fitts introduced the **"1 +" term** as a correction:

$$
ID = \log_2 \!\left( 1 + \dfrac{D}{W} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Assume Linear Relationship Between MT and ID

Empirically, **mean movement time (MT)** is linearly related to the index of difficulty:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute to Obtain Fitts' Law

$$
\boxed{\,MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( 1 + \dfrac{D}{W} \right)\,}
$$

**Explanation of each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= \text{Mean movement time to acquire the target (seconds)} \\
a &= \text{Intercept: device start/stop time, neural delay (typically } 50\text{–}200 \text{ ms)} \\
b &= \text{Slope: reciprocal of human information-processing rate (typically } 100\text{–}150 \text{ ms/bit)} \\
D &= \text{Distance from start point to target center (m or pixels)} \\
W &= \text{Effective target width measured along the axis of motion (m or pixels)} \\
ID &= \text{Index of difficulty in bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A pilot must move a joystick **25 cm** to actuate a button with an effective width of **2 cm**. Given $a = 0.1$ s and $b = 0.12$ s/bit, find $ID$ and $MT$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2 \!\left( 1 + \dfrac{D}{W} \right) \\
   &= \log_2 \!\left( 1 + \dfrac{25 \text{ cm}}{2 \text{ cm}} \right) \\
   &= \log_2 \!\left( 1 + 12.5 \right) \\
   &= \log_2(13.5) \\
   &\approx 3.755 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
   &= 0.1 \text{ s} + (0.12 \text{ s/bit})(3.755 \text{ bits}) \\
   &= 0.1 \text{ s} + 0.4506 \text{ s} \\
   &\approx 0.5506 \text{ s} \\
   &\approx 551 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The pilot requires approximately **3.76 bits of information processing** and **0.55 seconds** to acquire the target.

> [!NOTE] Implication for Movement Compatibility
> If the target width $W$ is increased (e.g., a larger button) — making the task **spatially more compatible** — the ID decreases and the MT decreases proportionally. This is the *mathematical proof* of why high spatial compatibility reduces workload.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Compatibility Types

| Compatibility Type | Driving Human Characteristic | Design Variable Tuned | Typical Metric | Common Error if Violated | KTU 2024 Weight |
|--------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------|----------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| Population Stereotype | Learned cultural expectation | Color, shape, label, symbol | Hit rate, RT | "Wrong-button" errors | **High** |
| Spatial | Spatial perception | Control–display position | Eye-scanning time | Cross-referencing errors | **High** |
| Movement | Kinesthetic / motor memory | Direction of control motion | Fitts' MT | Mode confusion | **High** |
| Conceptual | Mental model formation | Icons, metaphors, labels | Transfer-of-training time | Cognitive overhead | **Medium** |
| Sensory / Modality | Sensory channel capacity | Auditory vs. visual vs. tactile | Signal detection $d'$ | Missed signals | **Medium** |
| Cultural | Sociocultural norms | Color, language, iconography | User-satisfaction score | Cultural offense / confusion | **Medium** |
| Person–Machine (overall) | Integrated anthropometry, perception, cognition | Full workstation design | NASA-TLX, error rate | Cumulative fatigue / errors | **High** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Hick's Law (Choice Compatibility)

### Step 1 — Set Up the Choice Reaction Task

Hick (1952) measured reaction time $T$ as a function of the number of equally probable stimulus–response alternatives $N$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Logarithmic Relationship

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

This follows directly from information theory: choosing among $N$ equiprobable alternatives requires $\log_2(N)$ bits of decision information.

### Step 3 — Hyman Extension (Non-Equiprobable Stimuli)

If the stimuli have unequal probabilities $p_i$:

$$
T = a + b \cdot H
$$

where

$$
H = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2(p_i) \quad \text{(Shannon Entropy, in bits)}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

When **stimuli are predictably grouped** (high population stereotype compatibility), $H$ decreases, and so does $T$. Designers exploit this by *grouping, color-coding, and labeling* to reduce $H$ without removing functionality.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A nurse faces an infusion pump with **8** unlabeled buttons (random mapping). After ergonomic redesign, the buttons are grouped into **2 clusters of 4** semantically related buttons. Compare the reaction times using $a = 0.2$ s and $b = 0.15$ s/bit.

**Solution (Before redesign):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 0.2 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(8) \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.15 \cdot 3 \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.45 = 0.65 \text{ s} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (After redesign — within each cluster, the user first selects the cluster then the button; effective $N = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
